title: Operating Systems
description: Manual OS lecture and algorithm exam practice.

questions.prompt: What is the main role of an operating system?
choices: Compile every application into machine code
Manage hardware resources and provide services to programs
questions.explanation: An OS is the control program that manages hardware and offers services to application programs.

questions.prompt: Which OS goal focuses on making the computer easy for users and programs to use?
questions.explanation: Convenience means the OS hides hardware complexity and provides a usable environment.

questions.prompt: Which OS goal focuses on good use of CPU, memory, I/O devices, and storage?
questions.explanation: Resource utilization is about using hardware efficiently.

questions.prompt: Why does the OS use protection mechanisms?
choices: To force all programs to run as kernel code
To make every process share the same memory without limits
To prevent programs from interfering with each other or the system
questions.explanation: Protection prevents accidental or malicious misuse of resources.

questions.prompt: What is a system call?
choices: A controlled request from a program to the operating system
A direct edit to CPU hardware
questions.explanation: System calls are the interface used by programs to request OS services.

questions.prompt: Which mode should execute privileged OS kernel instructions?
questions.explanation: Privileged instructions run in kernel mode to protect the system.

exams.title: Lecture 2 - Processes, IPC, Synchronization, and Scheduling

questions.prompt: What is the best definition of a process?
choices: A program stored on disk but not running
A program in execution with its own state and resources
questions.explanation: A process is an executing program, including registers, memory, open files, and scheduling state.

questions.prompt: A process terminates because of normal exit, error exit, fatal error, or being killed. Which pairing is correct?
choices: A process can terminate only during shutdown
Normal exit and error exit are voluntary; fatal error and killed are involuntary
Only fatal error can terminate a process
questions.explanation: Normal/error exits are voluntary, while fatal errors and being killed are involuntary.

questions.prompt: A race condition occurs when the result depends on what?
choices: The exact timing or interleaving of execution
The size of the monitor screen
The number of files in a folder
questions.explanation: Race conditions happen when shared data is accessed concurrently and timing changes the result.

questions.prompt: What is mutual exclusion designed to protect?
choices: A critical region that accesses shared data
questions.explanation: Mutual exclusion allows only one process/thread at a time in a critical region.

questions.prompt: What is the key problem with pure busy waiting?
choices: It may waste CPU time while repeatedly checking a lock
It always prevents all race conditions automatically
questions.explanation: Spin waiting burns CPU while waiting for a condition to change.

questions.prompt: What does the Test-and-Set Lock instruction guarantee?
choices: It converts a semaphore into a file
It performs the lock read/write atomically
It removes every process from memory
It always schedules shortest jobs first
questions.explanation: TSL atomically reads a lock variable and stores a nonzero value so two CPUs cannot acquire it at the same time.

questions.prompt: What problem can occur with sleep and wakeup primitives?
questions.explanation: A wakeup signal can be lost if sent before the process is actually asleep.

questions.prompt: What does down(P) on a semaphore do when the value is zero?
questions.explanation: Down decrements a positive semaphore; if it is zero, the process blocks.

questions.prompt: What is priority inversion?
choices: A process changes from user mode to kernel mode
A high-priority task is blocked by a lower-priority task holding a needed resource
A low-priority task always finishes first by design
questions.explanation: Priority inversion happens when a lower-priority holder delays a higher-priority waiter.

questions.prompt: Which scheduling algorithm gives each ready process a time quantum?
questions.explanation: Round-Robin cycles through ready processes and gives each a quantum.

questions.prompt: Which process type has short CPU bursts and frequent I/O waits?
questions.explanation: I/O-bound processes often block for I/O and need quick response when ready.

questions.prompt: Which batch scheduling algorithm is optimal for minimizing average turnaround time when job lengths are known?
questions.explanation: SJF minimizes average turnaround time under the standard known-job-length assumption.

exams.title: Lecture 3 - Multiprocessors, Multicomputers, and Distributed Systems

questions.prompt: What is a multiprocessor system?
choices: A system that cannot run parallel work
A system used only for printing
A system with multiple CPUs sharing memory
A system with one CPU and no memory
questions.explanation: Multiprocessors contain multiple processors, often sharing a common memory space.

questions.prompt: What is a multicomputer system?
choices: Multiple computers connected by a network, usually with separate memories
A single process with many threads only
questions.explanation: Multicomputers communicate by messages because memory is not shared in the same way.

questions.prompt: Why is synchronization harder in distributed systems?
choices: There is no single global clock and communication has delays
Every process shares the same register
All nodes execute one instruction at a time
questions.explanation: Distributed systems must handle latency, partial failure, and lack of global time.

questions.prompt: What does transparency mean in a distributed system?
choices: Showing every packet to the user
Hiding distribution details from users or programs
questions.explanation: Transparency makes a distributed system appear simpler or more unified than it is.

questions.prompt: Which issue is a classic distributed-system failure problem?
choices: Every message is guaranteed before it is sent
Partial failure of one node while others continue
questions.explanation: Partial failures make distributed systems harder than centralized systems.

questions.prompt: What is virtual memory used for?
choices: Giving each process the illusion of a large private address space
Removing the need for secondary storage
Forcing all processes to share one address
questions.explanation: Virtual memory separates logical addresses from physical memory and supports isolation.

questions.prompt: What is a page fault?
choices: A referenced page is not currently in physical memory
questions.explanation: A page fault triggers OS handling to bring the needed page into memory if valid.

questions.prompt: What does FIFO page replacement remove?
choices: The page used farthest in the future
The page with the smallest file name
The page that has been in memory the longest
questions.explanation: FIFO removes the oldest loaded page, regardless of future use.

questions.prompt: Why is optimal page replacement not implementable in practice?
questions.explanation: Optimal replacement needs future knowledge, so it is mainly a benchmark.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of a working set model?
choices: Keep the pages a process is actively using in memory
questions.explanation: The working set approximates the locality currently needed by a process.

questions.prompt: What is a file system responsible for?
choices: Organizing, naming, storing, and retrieving files
questions.explanation: The file system manages persistent data and metadata.

questions.prompt: What does a directory mainly provide?
choices: A mapping from names to files or subdirectories
questions.explanation: Directories organize names and allow hierarchical file lookup.

questions.prompt: Why is caching used in file systems?
choices: To reduce repeated disk access and improve performance
To make files impossible to find
questions.explanation: Caching keeps frequently used blocks in memory.

questions.prompt: What problem can journaling help with?
choices: Recovering file-system consistency after a crash
questions.explanation: A journal records intended updates so recovery can repair incomplete operations.

questions.prompt: What is the fundamental idea of virtualization?
choices: Abstract the hardware of one computer into multiple execution environments.
Convert every program into a static library.
Replace the operating system with a file system.
Run only one process at a time.
questions.explanation: Virtualization creates virtual machines or environments on top of physical hardware.

questions.prompt: In virtualization terminology, what does the VMM or hypervisor do?
choices: Creates and runs virtual machines by providing a virtual hardware interface.
questions.explanation: The VMM controls the real machine and presents virtual machines to guests.

questions.prompt: What is usually running inside a VM as the guest?
choices: An operating system or application environment.
questions.explanation: Guests normally believe they are running on their own machine.

questions.prompt: What is a Type 0 hypervisor?
choices: An application running on top of a normal host OS.
A guest OS modified to call the hypervisor.
A hardware or firmware-based virtualization solution.
questions.explanation: Type 0 hypervisors are implemented by hardware/firmware features such as partitions.

questions.prompt: Which examples match Type 0 hypervisors from the lecture?
choices: BSD jails and Solaris zones only.
questions.explanation: LPARs and LDOMs are firmware/hardware-style partitioning systems.

questions.prompt: What is a Type 1 hypervisor?
choices: A normal desktop application that runs guests inside a host OS.
Operating-system-like software that runs directly on hardware to manage guests.
A programming language virtual machine only.
questions.explanation: Type 1 hypervisors provide virtualization directly on physical hardware.

questions.prompt: Which examples are Type 1 hypervisors or Type 1-style systems?
choices: VMware ESX, XenServer, Hyper-V, and KVM-based systems.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists dedicated and general-purpose OS variants as Type 1.

questions.prompt: What is a Type 2 hypervisor?
choices: Firmware that dedicates all hardware to partitions.
A CPU instruction used for binary translation.
A VMM application running on a standard host operating system.
questions.explanation: Type 2 hypervisors are hosted applications such as VMware Workstation or VirtualBox.

questions.prompt: Why are Type 2 hypervisors convenient for students or personal machines?
choices: They require no host OS changes and can run guests on Windows, Linux, or macOS.
They require replacing all hardware firmware.
They only work on IBM mainframes.
questions.explanation: Hosted hypervisors are easy to install on normal systems.

questions.prompt: What is paravirtualization?
choices: Exact hardware duplication with no guest changes ever.
A technique where the guest OS is modified to cooperate with the VMM for better performance.
Running a Java class file in a JVM.
questions.explanation: Paravirtualized guests use hypervisor-aware interfaces.

questions.prompt: Why is paravirtualization less needed on modern CPUs?
choices: Modern CPUs removed all privilege levels.
Hardware virtualization support reduced the need to modify guests.
Virtual machines can no longer use memory.
questions.explanation: Hardware support can handle operations that once required guest changes.

questions.prompt: What is programming-environment virtualization?
choices: Dedicating a physical GPU to one VM.
Using only trap-and-emulate for kernel code.
Providing a virtual execution environment and APIs for programs, such as JVM or .NET.
questions.explanation: This virtualizes a language/runtime environment instead of real hardware.

questions.prompt: What is emulation?
choices: Translating guest instructions so software for one CPU can run on a different CPU.
Running a guest only on identical underlying CPU hardware.
Sharing one file-system volume between guests.
Moving a VM live between hosts.
questions.explanation: Emulation allows cross-architecture execution but is much slower.

questions.prompt: Why is emulation usually slower than virtualization?
choices: It always has more physical CPUs than needed.
It must translate all guest CPU instructions to native CPU instructions.
questions.explanation: Instruction translation creates major overhead.

questions.prompt: What is application containment?
choices: Running a full guest kernel for every app.
Replacing every app with a hypervisor.
Segregating applications from the OS to provide virtualization-like isolation without full hardware virtualization.
questions.explanation: Zones and jails isolate applications while sharing the host kernel.

questions.prompt: How do containers or zones differ from full VMs?
choices: They always run a separate guest kernel per app.
They require a different CPU architecture.
They share one host kernel while giving applications isolated resources and namespaces.
questions.explanation: Application containment virtualizes OS views rather than full hardware.

questions.prompt: What formal condition says the VMM must control resources?
choices: The VMM is in complete control of system resources.
The guest can bypass the VMM for all devices.
The VMM should never trap privileged operations.
The host OS must be unaware of all guests.
questions.explanation: The classical definition includes control, equivalence, and efficiency.

questions.prompt: What is a VCPU?
choices: A physical CPU dedicated only to firmware.
A virtual representation of a guest CPU state.
questions.explanation: The VMM saves and restores VCPU state when scheduling guests.

questions.prompt: Why is virtualization difficult on a simple dual-mode CPU?
choices: There are too many privilege levels already.
A guest kernel cannot safely run in real kernel mode, so virtual kernel behavior must be simulated.
The CPU has no user mode.
questions.explanation: The VMM must protect itself while letting guest kernels believe they are privileged.

questions.prompt: What is trap-and-emulate?
choices: The VMM translates every user instruction even when not privileged.
The guest directly controls all physical devices.
The host OS ignores all guest errors.
Privileged guest operations trap to the VMM, which performs or simulates them and returns control.
questions.explanation: Trap-and-emulate is a core virtualization technique.

questions.prompt: Why does guest kernel code run slower with trap-and-emulate?
choices: Privileged operations cause traps and VMM handling overhead.
The guest has no virtual CPU.
questions.explanation: Kernel-like guest operations need VMM intervention.

questions.prompt: Why was binary translation needed on older x86 CPUs?
choices: x86 had no instructions at all.
Some sensitive instructions behaved differently without trapping in user mode.
Binary translation was used only for Java bytecode.
questions.explanation: Problematic instructions such as popf could not be handled by simple trap-and-emulate.

questions.prompt: How does binary translation handle guest kernel-mode code?
choices: It lets all instructions run in real kernel mode.
The VMM examines instructions and replaces problematic ones with safe sequences.
It disables the guest page tables.
questions.explanation: Binary translation rewrites sensitive code paths before execution.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of nested page tables?
choices: Represent guest page-table state while the VMM keeps control of real address translation.
questions.explanation: NPTs separate guest-visible physical memory from machine memory.

questions.prompt: What performance cost can nested page tables cause?
choices: More TLB misses and slower memory translation.
questions.explanation: Extra translation layers can increase TLB pressure.

questions.prompt: What did Intel VT-x and AMD-V add?
choices: A new file system for guest disks.
Hardware support with host and guest modes for virtualization.
A replacement for all device drivers.
questions.explanation: Hardware assistance reduces the need for binary translation.

questions.prompt: In hardware-assisted virtualization, what causes traps to the VMM?
choices: Access to virtualized devices or privileged instructions configured to exit guest mode.
questions.explanation: The CPU can run guests while trapping selected sensitive events.

questions.prompt: What is the typical VM lifecycle?
choices: Encrypt VM, print it, then archive the keyboard.
Boot firmware, format disk, remove CPU.
Only create a VM with no resource assignment.
Create VM, assign resources, run it, then delete it when no longer needed.
questions.explanation: VMs are managed objects with configurable CPU, memory, network, and storage.

questions.prompt: What is virtual machine sprawl?
choices: Too many VMs are created, making history and state difficult to track.
A VM uses too few resources.
A hypervisor cannot create any VM.
questions.explanation: Easy VM creation can lead to uncontrolled growth.

questions.prompt: Why is I/O challenging for Type 0 hypervisors?
choices: It is hard to dedicate enough physical devices and controllers to every guest.
questions.explanation: Shared I/O often needs a control partition or special design.

questions.prompt: What role can a control partition play in Type 0 systems?
choices: Translate Java bytecode into native code.
Run daemons that other guests communicate with for shared I/O.
questions.explanation: Control partitions help provide shared services.

questions.prompt: Why are Type 1 hypervisors common in datacenters?
choices: They run only as browser tabs.
They require one physical server per guest.
They consolidate many OSs and apps, support snapshots, cloning, and movement between hosts.
questions.explanation: Type 1 hypervisors act like datacenter operating systems.

questions.prompt: What is direct pass-through I/O?
choices: Giving a guest direct access to a physical device to reduce hypervisor involvement.
Copying every I/O byte through the VMM twice.
questions.explanation: Less hypervisor involvement can improve I/O performance.

questions.prompt: What does NAT provide for guest networking?
choices: It gives every guest a dedicated physical NIC.
It hides the guest local address by translating traffic through the VMM or host.
It moves memory pages to disk.
questions.explanation: NAT lets guests communicate using translated addresses.

questions.prompt: What is CPU overcommitment in virtualization?
choices: Every guest receives a dedicated physical CPU forever.
Configured VCPUs exceed available physical CPUs, so the VMM schedules them over time.
Only one VM can ever run.
questions.explanation: Overcommitment is common when not enough CPUs exist for all VCPUs.

questions.prompt: Why can CPU oversubscription break a guest time-sharing assumption?
choices: The guest scheduler always sees physical time exactly.
A guest 100 ms slice may take much longer in real time because the VMM steals cycles.
questions.explanation: VMM scheduling affects guest-visible timing.

questions.prompt: What is double paging?
choices: The guest thinks a page is in memory while the VMM has moved it to backing store.
Two guests share a CPU core.
A disk scheduler moves the head twice.
A Java object has two class files.
questions.explanation: Double paging can occur when both guest and VMM manage memory.

questions.prompt: What is a balloon memory manager?
choices: A pseudo-device driver inside the guest that allocates or releases memory under VMM direction.
questions.explanation: Ballooning helps the VMM reclaim or return guest memory.

questions.prompt: How can VMM page deduplication save memory?
choices: Every guest receives duplicated pages only.
All memory is encrypted and hidden.
Identical pages loaded in multiple guests can be mapped to the same physical page.
The VMM deletes guest page tables.
questions.explanation: Deduplication reduces memory use when guests share identical content.

questions.prompt: Why is storage for VMs usually represented as disk images?
choices: It requires one physical disk partition per guest always.
It allows guest disks and configuration to be stored as files or VMM-managed objects.
questions.explanation: Disk images make copying, moving, and creating guests easier.

questions.prompt: What is P-to-V conversion?
choices: Converting native physical disk blocks into a VMM virtual disk format.
Converting a Java class into bytecode.
Changing a Type 2 hypervisor into firmware.
Moving CPU time from guest to host.
questions.explanation: Physical-to-virtual conversion migrates existing systems into VM format.

questions.prompt: What is live migration?
choices: Deleting a VM and reinstalling it later.
Moving a running guest from one host to another without interrupting user access.
Running an emulator on a different CPU.
Creating a Java object at runtime.
questions.explanation: Live migration is a major VMM feature for maintenance and load balancing.

questions.prompt: What happens near the end of live migration?
choices: The guest is shut down for a full reinstall.
The source freezes the guest, sends final VCPU state and dirty pages, then the target starts it.
All pages are sent only after deleting the source.
questions.explanation: The final stop-and-copy phase transfers remaining changed state.

questions.prompt: What type of hypervisor is VMware Workstation?
choices: Type 2 hypervisor running as an application on a host OS.
questions.explanation: VMware Workstation runs on top of a native host OS.

questions.prompt: What is the JVM an example of?
questions.explanation: The JVM provides a virtual execution environment for Java bytecode.

questions.prompt: What does Java bytecode provide?
choices: Direct execution only on one CPU model.
Architecture-neutral code loaded and executed by a JVM built for the host architecture.
questions.explanation: Java programs compile to bytecode for portability.

questions.prompt: Why does the JVM use JIT compilation?
choices: To translate bytecode into native code and cache it for speed.
To dedicate a GPU to a VM.
questions.explanation: JIT improves runtime performance over pure interpretation.

questions.prompt: Which data type does a multimedia OS handle in addition to conventional files?
choices: Continuous media such as audio and video.
questions.explanation: Multimedia systems deal with ordinary files plus time-sensitive continuous media.

questions.prompt: Why is multimedia data difficult for an OS?
choices: It is always tiny and delay-insensitive.
It is large, high-rate, and sensitive to playback timing.
questions.explanation: Audio/video require timely delivery at high data rates.

questions.prompt: What is the primary OS challenge with continuous media?
choices: Real-time processing and delivery under timing constraints.
questions.explanation: Continuous media must arrive and be presented on time.

questions.prompt: How does multimedia differ from strict physical real-time control systems?
choices: Multimedia deadlines are always more catastrophic.
Physical control systems never care about timing.
A missed multimedia deadline may degrade quality, while a physical control miss may be catastrophic.
questions.explanation: Multimedia often tolerates occasional small deadline misses.

questions.prompt: Why is multimedia scheduling often simplified?
choices: All media tasks are random one-time jobs.
questions.explanation: Continuous media streams often have periodic processing demands.

questions.prompt: Which OS areas are key for multimedia support?
choices: Process management, file systems, synchronization, and memory management.
questions.explanation: Multimedia affects scheduling, disk layout, timing sync, and data movement.

questions.prompt: What is a deadline?
choices: The first time a process is created.
The size of a disk block.
The latest acceptable time to present or finish a processing result.
The number of users in a system.
questions.explanation: Deadlines define when media results must be ready.

questions.prompt: What is a soft deadline?
choices: A deadline that must never be missed under any condition.
A memory address used for buffers.
A deadline where occasional or small misses do not cause total failure.
questions.explanation: Video streaming commonly uses soft deadlines.

questions.prompt: What is a hard deadline?
choices: A deadline whose miss causes system failure.
A deadline used only for online video buffering.
A delay that is never checked.
A QoS value for file names.
questions.explanation: Hard deadlines are dictated by physical real-time processes.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of multimedia resource management?
choices: Allow every new connection regardless of capacity.
Maintain timing guarantees along the whole data path without violating existing connections.
Ignore existing streams when adding new ones.
questions.explanation: Resource managers must preserve guarantees for active streams.

questions.prompt: What is an active resource?
choices: A passive storage capacity such as file-system space only.
A resource that provides a service, such as CPU or a network adapter.
questions.explanation: Active resources perform work and are often exclusive.

questions.prompt: What is a passive resource?
choices: A required system capability such as memory or a file system.
questions.explanation: Passive resources support active resources and are usually more shareable.

questions.prompt: Which QoS parameter is determined by data rate and data-unit size?
questions.explanation: Throughput describes how much data must be processed or transferred.

questions.prompt: What does local delay measure?
choices: Total transmission from source to destination.
The maximum time a specific resource may take to complete a task.
The number of media files in storage.
The amount of RAM in a PC.
questions.explanation: Local delay is per-resource timing.

questions.prompt: What does end-to-end delay measure?
choices: Total time from source to destination.
questions.explanation: End-to-end delay covers the whole path.

questions.prompt: What is delay jitter?
choices: The allowed variance in data arrival time.
questions.explanation: Jitter measures timing variation, important for smooth playback.

questions.prompt: Why is forward error correction preferred for time-critical media?
choices: Retransmission may arrive too late to be useful.
It always reduces bandwidth to zero.
questions.explanation: FEC avoids waiting for retransmission during playback.

questions.prompt: What happens during resource negotiation?
choices: The OS immediately accepts all streams.
The client gives QoS needs and the resource manager tests whether capacity exists.
The file system deletes old streams.
questions.explanation: Admission control checks whether guarantees can be met.

questions.prompt: What are the resource management phases?
choices: Scheduling, QoS computation, and resource reservation.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these three phases.

questions.prompt: What is the pessimistic allocation approach?
choices: Reserve based only on average workload.
Reserve for the worst case to avoid conflicts, at the cost of low utilization.
questions.explanation: Pessimistic schemes are conservative.

questions.prompt: What is the optimistic allocation approach?
choices: Reserve based on average workload and monitor overload situations.
questions.explanation: Optimistic schemes improve utilization but need overload handling.

questions.prompt: What does the continuous media resource model use as stream units?
questions.explanation: LDUs model media stream units along an end-to-end path.

questions.prompt: Which parameters define LBAP?
choices: Maximum Burstiness, Maximum Message Rate, and Maximum Message Size.
questions.explanation: LBAP uses B, R, and M.

questions.prompt: What is the LBAP buffer-size formula?
questions.explanation: The formula sizes buffers for bursty arrivals.

questions.prompt: What is logical backlog?
choices: The number of messages ahead of schedule when a message arrives.
The number of failed login attempts.
The number of mounted file systems.
questions.explanation: Logical backlog tracks work that arrives early.

questions.prompt: When is a message considered critical in the workahead model?
choices: When its logical arrival time has passed.
When it is a text file.
questions.explanation: Once logical arrival time passes, delay threatens playback.

questions.prompt: What does the dispatcher do in process management?
choices: Maps ready processes to resources according to scheduling policy.
questions.explanation: The dispatcher is the kernel component that selects what runs.

questions.prompt: What are the multimedia scheduling goals?
choices: Run only one stream at a time.
Avoid starvation for uncritical work and prevent priority inversion for time-critical processes.
questions.explanation: Both critical and noncritical work must be managed properly.

questions.prompt: Which two basic real-time scheduling algorithms are named?
choices: Earliest Deadline First and Rate Monotonic Scheduling.
questions.explanation: EDF and RM are the lecture basic algorithms.

questions.prompt: What parameters define a scheduling task in the system model?
choices: Start time, processing time, deadline, and period.
questions.explanation: Tasks are described as (s, e, d, p).

questions.prompt: What is processor utilization for task i?
choices: Message size divided by disk size.
Processing time divided by period, ei/pi.
questions.explanation: Utilization measures CPU demand relative to available period.

questions.prompt: How does EDF choose priority?
choices: The task with the largest file name runs first.
The task with the lowest data rate runs first always.
The newest task always runs first.
The task with the nearest deadline gets highest priority.
questions.explanation: EDF is dynamic and deadline-driven.

questions.prompt: Why can EDF behave poorly during overload?
choices: It has no absolute guarantee when total demand exceeds capacity.
questions.explanation: EDF is optimal under feasible loads but not magic under overload.

questions.prompt: How does the TDS extension help EDF overload?
choices: It terminates jobs that can no longer finish before deadline.
It stores all media in one block.
questions.explanation: TDS avoids wasting time on impossible jobs.

questions.prompt: Why are mandatory and optional task parts useful?
choices: Mandatory parts are always cancelled first.
Optional parts can be cancelled during overload, preserving essential output.
They are used only for disk formatting.
questions.explanation: Scalable media can reduce quality by dropping optional work.

questions.prompt: How does Rate Monotonic assign priority?
choices: Higher request rate means higher static priority.
questions.explanation: RM is static and rate-based.

questions.prompt: What assumption is required for Rate Monotonic scheduling?
choices: Tasks are all nonperiodic and dependent.
All tasks have zero processing time.
questions.explanation: RM works for periodic independent task sets.

questions.prompt: What does the RM utilization bound indicate?
choices: If disk seek time is zero, all files are contiguous.
If total ei/pi is below the bound, all deadlines are guaranteed.
If passwords are salted, CPU is fair.
If jitter is high, all streams succeed.
questions.explanation: The bound is a schedulability test.

questions.prompt: What is Least Laxity First based on?
choices: Slack time: deadline minus current time minus remaining processing time.
questions.explanation: LLF dynamically prioritizes the least slack.

questions.prompt: Why is LLF difficult to implement?
choices: It only works for file compression.
questions.explanation: Accurate remaining time is hard to know.

questions.prompt: Why can preemptive scheduling reduce priority inversion?
choices: Higher-priority work can interrupt lower-priority work.
It forces all work to finish in arrival order.
questions.explanation: Preemption lets urgent tasks regain the resource.

questions.prompt: Which service is required from multimedia file systems?
choices: Constant and timely retrieval of digitized video and audio.
questions.explanation: Media playback depends on timely disk service.

questions.prompt: What is a problem with contiguous storage?
choices: It always makes random access impossible.
Expanding a file can require moving it and causes external fragmentation.
questions.explanation: Contiguous storage is fast but inflexible.

questions.prompt: What is a problem with non-sequential storage?
choices: Random access can be costly because prior blocks or metadata paths may be needed.
questions.explanation: Non-contiguous layouts can hurt media streaming.

questions.prompt: Which disk access-time factor is usually largest?
questions.explanation: Moving the disk head is usually the biggest mechanical delay.

questions.prompt: What does SSTF disk scheduling choose?
choices: The request with the shortest seek time from the current head position.
questions.explanation: SSTF reduces seek time but may starve edge requests.

questions.prompt: How does C-SCAN differ from SCAN?
choices: C-SCAN never moves the disk head.
C-SCAN serves in one direction then returns to the start for fairer service.
questions.explanation: Circular SCAN makes wait times more uniform.

questions.prompt: Why can plain disk EDF perform poorly for multimedia?
choices: It ignores physical head position, causing excessive seek time.
questions.explanation: Deadline order alone may destroy disk throughput.

questions.prompt: What does SCAN-EDF combine?
choices: Earliest deadline ordering with SCAN direction for equal-deadline requests.
questions.explanation: SCAN-EDF balances timing and disk movement efficiency.

questions.prompt: How does Group Sweeping Scheduling work?
choices: All requests are served strictly by shortest seek.
Streams are grouped by similar deadlines; groups are served in order and requests inside a group use SCAN.
Every stream gets a separate disk.
questions.explanation: GSS reduces seek overhead while respecting deadline groups.

questions.prompt: What is the mixed disk scheduling strategy based on?
choices: A balance strategy and shortest seek.
questions.explanation: Mixed scheduling changes behavior based on urgency.

questions.prompt: In the mixed strategy, when should the balance strategy be used?
choices: When urgency is small and buffers are full enough.
Only when no media stream exists.
When urgency is large and buffers are close to running out.
questions.explanation: High urgency means streams need data soon.

questions.prompt: In the mixed strategy, when is shortest seek safe?
questions.explanation: Low urgency gives room to optimize disk movement.

questions.prompt: Which definition best matches an embedded system?
choices: Electronics and software inside a product to perform a specific function
A general-purpose desktop designed mainly for many unrelated user tasks
A cloud platform that only rents virtual machines
A compiler feature that converts source code to assembly
questions.explanation: An embedded system is computing hardware and software built into a product for a dedicated function.

questions.prompt: In embedded-system organization, which pair represents external interaction with the environment?
choices: Sensors as input and actuators as output
questions.explanation: Sensors detect environmental input, and actuators perform output actions.

questions.prompt: Why are many embedded systems described as reactive?
choices: They execute only after a human opens a desktop application
They never receive input from sensors
Their execution pace is often determined by events in the environment
They run only batch jobs with no external timing
questions.explanation: Reactive systems respond to outside events such as sensor changes or control signals.

questions.prompt: Which set of requirements is most typical for embedded systems?
choices: Maximum graphics quality, unlimited RAM, and no time constraints
Only database throughput and user account management
Large desktop applications and frequent manual maintenance
Energy efficiency, small code size, limited memory, and high reliability
questions.explanation: Embedded systems usually have strict limits on power, memory, size, timing, and reliability.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between an application processor and a dedicated processor?
choices: An application processor can run complex OSs, while a dedicated processor is specialized for a specific task
A dedicated processor always runs a full desktop operating system
An application processor has no memory access
A dedicated processor is used only for cloud storage
questions.explanation: Application processors are high-end and general within the device, while dedicated processors are optimized for special functions.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes a microprocessor from a microcontroller?
choices: A microprocessor always includes all RAM, ROM, and I/O on the same chip
A microcontroller integrates processor, memory, and I/O logic in one package
A microprocessor is used only inside wireless sensors
questions.explanation: Microcontrollers integrate the processor, memory, and I/O logic in one package.

questions.prompt: Why are microcontrollers produced in extremely large numbers?
choices: They are useful only for laptop CPUs
They provide low-cost, low-area, energy-efficient control for many products
They require a full human interface in every device
questions.explanation: Microcontrollers are cheap, small, and efficient, so they appear in cars, toys, appliances, and many other products.

questions.prompt: Which description best fits a deeply embedded system?
choices: A desktop machine used for software development
A public cloud service sold to many customers
A database server with many human users
A single-purpose device that senses environmental data and performs basic processing
questions.explanation: Deeply embedded systems are often tiny, single-purpose, networked sensing or control devices.

questions.prompt: Which constraint is common in deeply embedded systems?
choices: Limited RAM and strict power management needs
questions.explanation: Deeply embedded devices often run on batteries with limited memory and processing power.

questions.prompt: Which feature belongs to an embedded OS more than a general desktop OS?
choices: Only support for unlimited user-installed applications
Direct use of interrupts and high configurability for a specific device
Mandatory paging even when hardware has no MMU
questions.explanation: Embedded OSs are often configurable, reactive, interrupt-driven, and tailored to device hardware.

questions.prompt: Why might a complex embedded product need an embedded OS instead of a simple loop?
choices: To make the device behave like a word processor
To manage multiple tasks, timing, interrupts, and device interactions
questions.explanation: As embedded software becomes complex, an OS helps coordinate tasks and hardware events.

questions.prompt: In embedded development, what are the host and target environments?
choices: The host is the final embedded device, and the target is the developer PC
Both host and target are always the same microcontroller
The host is a network switch, and the target is always a printer
The host is the development machine, and the target is the embedded device that runs the code
questions.explanation: Code is usually written and built on a powerful host, then run on a smaller target device.

questions.prompt: What is the role of a cross-compiler in embedded systems?
choices: It produces executable code for the target architecture while running on the host
It schedules cloud VMs across datacenters
It replaces the boot loader after every interrupt
questions.explanation: A cross-compiler is needed when the development host and embedded target use different architectures.

questions.prompt: What does a boot loader usually do in an embedded system?
choices: Provide all user applications in the cloud
Initialize hardware and load the kernel into RAM
Replace every device driver with a database
questions.explanation: The boot loader is the early startup program that prepares hardware and loads the OS kernel.

questions.prompt: Why are embedded kernels usually built with only necessary modules?
choices: To increase kernel size as much as possible
To force every device to use virtual memory
To save memory and remove features unsupported by the hardware
To make all drivers optional at runtime only
questions.explanation: Unneeded modules such as paging on non-MMU hardware waste space and may not work.

questions.prompt: What is a disadvantage of adapting a commercial OS for embedded use?
choices: It can never provide familiar interfaces
It cannot support any device driver
It may be too large, slower, or less predictable for real-time behavior
questions.explanation: Stripped-down commercial OSs can be familiar but may still carry size and timing costs.

questions.prompt: Which property is typical of a purpose-built embedded OS?
choices: Fast thread switching, small size, and deterministic interrupt response
Slow thread switching and unpredictable interrupt response
Mandatory large swap files on disk
No support for locking code or data in memory
questions.explanation: Purpose-built embedded OSs are designed for small size and predictable timing.

questions.prompt: Which requirement belongs to a real-time embedded kernel?
choices: Bounded primitive execution, real-time queues, clocks, alarms, and time-outs
Only best-effort scheduling with no clock
questions.explanation: Real-time kernels must provide predictable timing and mechanisms for timed control.

questions.prompt: What is embedded Linux?
choices: A Linux system that always requires a desktop GPU
A version of Linux that cannot use device drivers
A Linux system tailored for embedded devices, often using a custom kernel configuration
A Linux system used only as a web browser
questions.explanation: Embedded Linux is Linux adapted and configured for device constraints.

questions.prompt: Which factor is used to classify embedded Linux systems in the slides?
choices: Only the number of desktop users
Only the color of the device screen
Only the number of installed office programs
Kernel and memory size, timing constraints, networkability, and user interaction level
questions.explanation: The lecture classifies embedded Linux by size, timing, connectivity, and interface complexity.

questions.prompt: Why must embedded Linux kernels often be aggressively stripped down?
choices: Linux kernel size has grown over time while embedded devices may have limited memory
Embedded systems always have unlimited flash storage
All embedded devices require full desktop services
questions.explanation: Kernel growth conflicts with embedded memory and storage limits, so unused features are removed.

questions.prompt: What storage medium do embedded Linux file systems often target?
choices: Flash memory rather than traditional hard disks
questions.explanation: Embedded systems commonly use flash, which needs specialized file-system behavior.

questions.prompt: Which file system is log-based and designed for NOR and NAND flash with wear leveling?
questions.explanation: JFFS2 is the journaling flash file system used for flash devices and wear leveling.

questions.prompt: Which file-system pair is mainly used for compressed read-only embedded file systems?
questions.explanation: cramfs and squashfs are compressed read-only file systems used to save space.

questions.prompt: Which advantage is associated with embedded Linux?
choices: Vendor independence and broad hardware support
Support for only one processor family
questions.explanation: Embedded Linux benefits include low cost, open source, vendor independence, and hardware support.

questions.prompt: What is mClinux designed for?
choices: Only desktop computers with large swap files
Processors without a memory management unit
questions.explanation: mClinux is a Linux variation for microcontrollers and systems without an MMU.

questions.prompt: What memory-management limitation exists in mClinux compared with full Linux?
choices: Each process gets a protected virtual address space
mClinux requires more than 32 GB RAM
All processes share a single address space and applications may need absolute linking
questions.explanation: Without an MMU, mClinux lacks full virtual address spaces and process isolation.

questions.prompt: Why does mClinux use vfork() instead of fork()?
choices: Because vfork() is a networking protocol
Because fork() only works in TinyOS
Because vfork() always creates a cloud VM
Because fork() depends on separate address-space support not available without an MMU
questions.explanation: vfork() lets the child borrow the parent address space until execve() or exit().

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of mClibc?
choices: To provide a smaller C library suitable for embedded Linux systems
To replace flash storage with cloud storage
To force every embedded system to use glibc
questions.explanation: mClibc reduces redundancy and disables features by default to save space.

questions.prompt: What kind of protection can newer mClinux versions provide with an MPU?
choices: Full virtual memory identical to a desktop MMU
Basic process-to-kernel protection with less hardware overhead than a full MMU
questions.explanation: An MPU gives simpler protection than a full MMU but can still separate process and kernel access.

questions.prompt: What is TinyOS mainly designed for?
choices: Wireless sensor networks and very small embedded systems
questions.explanation: TinyOS targets tiny, power-constrained sensor devices with minimal memory.

questions.prompt: Which statement about TinyOS is correct?
choices: It requires gigabytes of RAM to boot
It is designed only for graphical smartphones
It cannot be used with wireless sensor networks
It is a very minimal OS focused on power efficiency and simplicity rather than being a full real-time OS
questions.explanation: TinyOS is minimal and power-aware, with very small code and memory needs.

questions.prompt: What workload pattern is common in wireless sensor networks?
choices: Sensors are off most of the time, with low-rate data and many concurrent events
Sensors are active at full power all the time
Every sensor runs a full desktop application
questions.explanation: Wireless sensor networks save power by sleeping often and reacting to events.

questions.prompt: Which list matches TinyOS component abstractions?
choices: Processes, page tables, and swap partitions
questions.explanation: TinyOS components are built around commands, events, and tasks.

questions.prompt: In TinyOS, what is the difference between commands and events?
choices: Commands are upward signals, and events are downward calls
Commands and events are both disk blocks
Commands are downward calls to lower components, while events are upward signals from lower components
Events are used only for file compression
questions.explanation: Commands go down the component stack, and events signal upward, often from hardware interrupts.

questions.prompt: How do TinyOS tasks execute?
choices: They require one kernel stack per task like a full desktop OS
They are cloud services launched by Nova
They are atomic, run to completion, and do not preempt each other
questions.explanation: TinyOS tasks are nonpreemptive and run to completion, which reduces stack complexity.

questions.prompt: Which concurrency rule is used by TinyOS?
choices: Events can preempt tasks, but tasks cannot preempt each other
Tasks can preempt events, but events never interrupt tasks
All components run as independent Linux processes
Only one event can ever occur in the whole system
questions.explanation: TinyOS has two concurrency levels: tasks and events.

questions.prompt: What does nesC add for TinyOS component specification?
choices: A virtual machine monitor for IaaS clouds
A component model with provided and used interfaces plus event-based concurrency
A replacement for all embedded hardware
questions.explanation: TinyOS uses nesC to define components, interfaces, and event-driven behavior.

questions.prompt: How does the TinyOS scheduler behave when the task queue is empty?
choices: It enters a low-power sleep mode
It runs all tasks in parallel
questions.explanation: The scheduler is a simple FIFO queue and saves energy by sleeping when there is no work.

questions.prompt: What does wiring mean in TinyOS?
choices: Connecting a component that uses an interface to one that provides it
questions.explanation: Wiring connects components at compile time through interfaces.

questions.prompt: Which TinyOS resource type is accessed through an arbiter because it must be shared safely?
questions.explanation: Shared resources use an arbiter to enforce mutual exclusion and manage requests.

questions.prompt: What does a TinyOS arbiter do for a shared resource?
choices: Allows all clients to use the resource at the same instant
Chooses which client may lock, use, and release the resource, while queueing others
Converts the resource into a cloud storage object
questions.explanation: The arbiter enforces the sharing policy and ensures one client uses the resource at a time.

questions.prompt: Which TinyOS shared-resource interface is used by a client to request and release access?
questions.explanation: The Resource interface handles access requests and release commands.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the Resource Configure interface in TinyOS?
choices: Automatically configure the resource just before a client is granted access
questions.explanation: Resource Configure prepares a resource for a client before access is granted.

questions.prompt: Which statement best defines cloud computing?
choices: Ubiquitous, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable resources
A fixed local machine with no network services
A storage device used only inside one embedded sensor
A scheduling algorithm for one CPU core
questions.explanation: Cloud computing provides elastic network access to shared resources such as servers, storage, applications, and services.

questions.prompt: What does on-demand self-service mean in cloud computing?
choices: Consumers must call an administrator for every resource change
Consumers can provision computing capabilities automatically without provider human interaction
Only the provider can start or stop resources manually
Resources are assigned once and never changed
questions.explanation: On-demand self-service lets consumers request capabilities automatically as needed.

questions.prompt: Which essential characteristic means cloud capabilities are reachable through standard network mechanisms?
questions.explanation: Broad network access means access through standard mechanisms from clients such as laptops and phones.

questions.prompt: What does resource pooling mean?
choices: Every consumer owns a physically separate datacenter
Only one application can use the cloud at a time
Resources are never reassigned between customers
Provider resources serve multiple consumers through a multitenant model
questions.explanation: Resource pooling uses shared provider resources to serve multiple consumers.

questions.prompt: Which characteristic allows cloud resources to scale outward and inward quickly with demand?
questions.explanation: Rapid elasticity is the ability to provision and release resources quickly.

questions.prompt: Why is measured service important?
choices: It disables all billing and monitoring
It automatically controls, optimizes, and meters resource use for transparency
It prevents users from seeing any usage data
It removes the need for resource allocation
questions.explanation: Measured service gives both provider and consumer visibility into resource use.

questions.prompt: Which service model lets consumers use provider-hosted applications without managing the underlying infrastructure?
questions.explanation: SaaS exposes applications while the provider manages the cloud infrastructure.

questions.prompt: Which service model lets consumers deploy applications using provider-supported languages, libraries, and tools?
questions.explanation: PaaS gives a platform for deploying consumer-created or acquired applications.

questions.prompt: Which service model gives consumers control over operating systems, storage, and deployed applications?
questions.explanation: IaaS provides fundamental resources while consumers manage their OSs and applications.

questions.prompt: Which deployment model is operated solely for one organization and offers the highest control?
questions.explanation: A private cloud is dedicated to one organization and may be managed internally or by a third party.

questions.prompt: Which deployment model is shared by organizations with common concerns?
questions.explanation: A community cloud supports a specific community of organizations with shared requirements.

questions.prompt: What is a hybrid cloud?
choices: A single cloud that cannot connect to any other model
A SaaS application with no infrastructure
A storage protocol for block devices
A composition of two or more distinct cloud deployment models
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private, public, or community clouds with portability mechanisms.

questions.prompt: Which list matches the five major NIST cloud actors?
questions.explanation: NIST identifies cloud consumer, provider, broker, auditor, and carrier.

questions.prompt: What is the role of a cloud service consumer?
choices: To only audit performance with no service use
To maintain a business relationship with and use services from cloud providers
To transport packets without owning any relationship
To replace the cloud provider hardware
questions.explanation: The consumer uses cloud services and has a business relationship with providers.

questions.prompt: What is the role of a cloud service provider?
choices: To only connect network links between users
To make cloud services available to interested parties
To choose services from many providers for a consumer
questions.explanation: The provider is responsible for making services available.

questions.prompt: Which actor conducts unbiased assessments such as security and performance audits?
questions.explanation: A cloud auditor independently evaluates cloud services.

questions.prompt: What does a cloud carrier provide?
choices: Connectivity and transport of cloud services between providers and consumers
Application source code written by the consumer
questions.explanation: The carrier provides the network transport path for cloud services.

questions.prompt: What is service intermediation by a cloud broker?
choices: Combining flash file systems in embedded Linux
Adding value such as identity management, reporting, or enhanced security
questions.explanation: Intermediation adds extra capability on top of a cloud service.

questions.prompt: What is service aggregation by a cloud broker?
choices: Selecting exactly one provider and ignoring cost
Auditing the provider without managing delivery
Combining multiple cloud services to meet consumer needs or optimize performance/cost
Transporting packets across a carrier network only
questions.explanation: Aggregation combines several services into a useful package for the consumer.

questions.prompt: What is service arbitrage by a cloud broker?
choices: Forcing all consumers onto one fixed provider
Measuring processor usage for billing only
Using multiple possible providers and choosing among them flexibly
questions.explanation: Arbitrage lets the broker choose among services from different providers.

questions.prompt: Which cloud networking type consists of racks of servers and storage connected by high-speed top-of-rack switches?
questions.explanation: Datacenter networks connect provider servers and storage with high-speed switching.

questions.prompt: What concern is central to cloud carriers in networking?
choices: Replacing all virtual machines with physical desktops
Creating virtual networks with suitable SLA and security guarantees
questions.explanation: Cloud carriers must provide secure, reliable network transport with appropriate guarantees.

questions.prompt: What is a Cloud Operating System?
choices: A mobile phone OS used only for local apps
A file system for flash memory only
A distributed OS in a provider datacenter that manages servers, network, and storage
A CPU scheduling policy for one desktop
questions.explanation: A Cloud OS manages physical and virtualized datacenter resources and implements IaaS.

questions.prompt: Why is a Cloud OS distinct from PaaS?
choices: It only supplies programming libraries to application developers
It is never used in datacenters
It cannot manage storage or networks
It manages physical and virtualized hardware transparently to the end user
questions.explanation: Cloud OS is the infrastructure management layer, while PaaS is an application platform layer.

questions.prompt: Which operation belongs to the computing service requirement for IaaS?
choices: Creating, deleting, starting, shutting down, suspending, or migrating VMs
questions.explanation: IaaS computing services manage VM lifecycle operations.

questions.prompt: Which storage types must an IaaS storage service provide?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists block, file, and object storage as IaaS storage services.

questions.prompt: Which network functions are part of IaaS network service?
choices: IP addresses, VLANs, virtual switches, load balancers, and firewalls
questions.explanation: IaaS networking provides virtual network functions for cloud resources.

questions.prompt: What does virtualization enable in the general architecture of a Cloud OS?
choices: Forcing every server to run one fixed application
Decoupling software from hardware so capacity can scale with virtual resources
questions.explanation: Virtualization lets providers move from dedicated servers to flexible COTS hardware and virtual capacity.

questions.prompt: In cloud infrastructure, what does SAN provide?
choices: Block-level access to storage over a high-speed network
File sharing only through a network appliance
Object metadata with unique IDs only
questions.explanation: A SAN provides block-level storage access through a high-speed network.

questions.prompt: How does object storage organize data?
choices: As objects containing data, metadata, and a unique identifier
As one local directory tree with no metadata
questions.explanation: Object storage stores data with metadata and unique IDs, supporting massive scalability.

questions.prompt: What is MANO responsible for?
choices: Replacing all cloud storage with flash memory
Composing and coordinating system components and managing lifecycle operations
questions.explanation: Management and Orchestration coordinates compute, storage, network, and service lifecycle tasks.

questions.prompt: What does the VM Manager do in MANO?
choices: Provides a compressed read-only file system
Oversees the creation and management of VM instances
questions.explanation: The VM Manager handles virtual-machine instance management.

questions.prompt: What is OpenStack in this lecture?
choices: A modular open-source project for building a Cloud OS
A compressed embedded Linux file system
questions.explanation: OpenStack is presented as a case study for an open-source Cloud OS.

questions.prompt: Which OpenStack component manages compute instances?
questions.explanation: Nova manages the lifecycle of compute instances.

questions.prompt: Which statement about Nova is correct?
choices: Nova includes all virtualization software itself
Nova is only an object store for static data
Nova manages underlying virtualization mechanisms through drivers but does not include the virtualization software itself
questions.explanation: Nova works with hypervisors through drivers rather than being the hypervisor itself.

questions.prompt: Which Nova component decides where a requested VM instance should run?
questions.explanation: The Nova Scheduler places VM instance requests.

questions.prompt: What does Glance provide in OpenStack?
choices: A library of virtual disk images that users can discover, register, and retrieve
Persistent block volumes attached to guest VMs
questions.explanation: Glance is OpenStack Image Service.

questions.prompt: What does Neutron provide?
choices: Networking as a service, letting users create networks and attach interfaces
questions.explanation: Neutron provides OpenStack networking services and plugin-based extensibility.

questions.prompt: Which pairing is correct for Swift and Cinder?
choices: Swift provides identity; Cinder provides dashboard UI
Swift schedules VMs; Cinder stores VM images
Swift provides object storage; Cinder provides persistent block storage volumes
Swift manages interrupts; Cinder manages TinyOS tasks
questions.explanation: Swift is object storage, while Cinder provides block volumes for guest VMs.

questions.prompt: What does Keystone provide?
choices: A web UI for launching instances only
A central directory of users and common authentication for OpenStack services
questions.explanation: Keystone is OpenStack Identity.

questions.prompt: Which services coordinate when launching a VM according to the slides?
choices: Keystone for credentials, Nova Scheduler for placement, and Glance for the disk image
questions.explanation: Launching a VM requires identity, placement, and image services, with storage if needed.

questions.prompt: What does Horizon provide?
choices: A web-based interface for managing cloud infrastructure
A network switch inside a rack only
questions.explanation: Horizon is the OpenStack dashboard.

questions.prompt: What is Ceilometer used for?
choices: Metering usage data for billing, benchmarking, scalability, and statistics
questions.explanation: Ceilometer collects metering data such as CPU, storage, and network usage.

questions.prompt: What does Heat do in OpenStack?
choices: Stores static data as objects only
Orchestrates composite cloud applications from templates across their lifecycle
questions.explanation: Heat is OpenStack orchestration and uses templates as infrastructure code.

questions.prompt: Which optional OpenStack service provides database-as-a-service?
questions.explanation: Trove provisions relational and nonrelational database engines.

questions.prompt: Which optional OpenStack service stores and manages secrets such as passwords and encryption keys?
questions.explanation: Barbican provides secure storage and management for secret values.

questions.prompt: What is the Internet of Things?
choices: A single centralized mainframe with no devices
A type of virtual disk image only
A computing and communications model involving many interconnected devices with sensors and actuators
questions.explanation: IoT connects everyday objects, sensors, actuators, and cloud services.

questions.prompt: Which components are typical in an IoT-enabled device?
choices: Sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, transceivers, and sometimes RFID
questions.explanation: IoT devices sense, act, process locally, and communicate wirelessly.

questions.prompt: What are the four enterprise IoT context layers in the slides?
questions.explanation: The lecture places IoT in Edge, Fog, Core, and Cloud layers.

questions.prompt: What happens at the Edge layer?
choices: Sensors and actuators interact with the environment
questions.explanation: The edge is closest to the physical environment.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of fog computing?
choices: Store all data only in far cloud servers before any filtering
Replace every IoT device with a desktop
Move processing closer to sensors for data reduction and real-time analysis
questions.explanation: Fog computing reduces latency and data volume before reaching the cloud.

questions.prompt: What does the Core layer do in the IoT context?
choices: Provides the high-performance backbone connecting fog to cloud
questions.explanation: The core connects fog systems with cloud systems.

questions.prompt: How does fog differ from cloud in the slide comparison?
choices: Fog is distributed at the edge with low latency; cloud is centralized with higher latency
Fog is always more centralized than cloud
Cloud only serves tens of devices, while fog serves one user
Fog has no relation to IoT devices
questions.explanation: Fog is closer to devices and optimized for low latency, while cloud is centralized and highly available.

questions.prompt: Why do many IoT devices need a specialized OS?
choices: They always have unlimited datacenter resources
They have tighter RAM, ROM, and power constraints than smartphones
questions.explanation: IoT OSs must fit constrained hardware and conserve power.

questions.prompt: Which constrained-device class has memory less than 10 kB and code less than 100 kB?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists Class 0 as the smallest constrained-device class.

questions.prompt: Which key IoT OS characteristic supports many different 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit microcontrollers?
questions.explanation: IoT operating systems must run across many microcontroller architectures.

questions.prompt: Which characteristic is critical for battery-powered IoT devices expected to last for years?
questions.explanation: Battery-powered IoT devices depend on low-power operation.

questions.prompt: Which layers/components are in a typical IoT OS architecture?
choices: HAL, kernel, system libraries, device drivers/logical file system, and network stack
Only sensors and actuators with no software layers
questions.explanation: The lecture architecture includes hardware abstraction, kernel services, libraries, drivers/file system, and networking.

questions.prompt: What does the Hardware Abstraction Layer do?
choices: Presents a consistent API to upper layers while mapping operations to specific hardware
questions.explanation: The HAL hides hardware differences behind common interfaces.

questions.prompt: Which interface is a generic pin that can be set as input or output at run time?
questions.explanation: GPIO means General Purpose Input/Output.

questions.prompt: Which HAL interface is an asynchronous serial digital data link?
questions.explanation: UART is the Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter interface.

questions.prompt: Which HAL interface is a synchronous serial digital data link?
questions.explanation: SPI is the Serial Peripheral Interface.

questions.prompt: What does the IoT kernel typically provide?
choices: Scheduler, task management, mutual exclusion, and timers
questions.explanation: The IoT kernel provides minimal core OS services.

questions.prompt: Why must the IoT kernel be modular?
choices: To force every device to install every driver
To make all devices use the same memory size
To allow minimal configurations tailored to specific devices
questions.explanation: Modularity lets an IoT OS include only what the device needs.

questions.prompt: Why do many constrained IoT devices use a low-power network stack?
choices: They may need limited sensor-to-gateway communication or efficient end-to-end Internet communication
They always transmit unlimited video to the cloud
They only communicate through local disk files
questions.explanation: Low-power networking matches limited energy and communication needs.

questions.prompt: What are examples of other main IoT OS components besides the kernel and network stack?
choices: Streamlined support libraries, device drivers, and a logical file system
questions.explanation: IoT OSs include minimal libraries, drivers, and file-system support configured for the application.

questions.prompt: What is RIOT designed for?
choices: Only full Linux servers with more than 32 GB RAM
questions.explanation: RIOT is an open-source OS specifically designed for constrained IoT devices.

questions.prompt: Which architecture does RIOT follow?
choices: Large monolithic desktop-only kernel with no drivers
Microkernel architecture with essential features in the core and other functions as threads
questions.explanation: RIOT keeps essential features in the core and runs drivers/libraries separately.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is correct?
choices: RIOT supports C/C++, multithreading, and real-time features with a small footprint
TinyOS has full standard C support and full multithreading like Linux
mClinux uses less memory than RIOT and TinyOS
RIOT requires more than 32 MB RAM in the lecture comparison
questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts RIOT with TinyOS and mClinux by footprint and features.

questions.prompt: How is RIOT structured?
choices: Core kernel, drivers, packages, and system libraries
Only commands, events, and TinyOS wiring
Only block, file, and object storage
questions.explanation: RIOT is organized into core, drivers, pkg, and sys.

questions.prompt: Which modules are included in the RIOT kernel core?
choices: SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, and public cloud
IRQ handling, IPC, scheduling, synchronization, and threads
Sensors, actuators, RFID, and fog only
questions.explanation: The RIOT kernel core includes the OS mechanisms needed for concurrent IoT programs.

questions.prompt: What does RIOT IPC provide?
choices: A safe, synchronized way for threads to communicate with defined priorities
questions.explanation: RIOT IPC is for synchronized communication between threads.

questions.prompt: What does RIOT power management handle?
questions.explanation: RIOT includes a kernel power-management module for low-power devices.

questions.prompt: What does the RIOT hardware abstraction relationship mean?
choices: Every processor belongs to only one board design forever
A board has exactly one processor, while a processor can be part of many boards
Hardware abstraction is unrelated to portability
questions.explanation: RIOT separates processor and board support to improve portability.

questions.prompt: What does scheduling in RIOT support?
choices: Only one nonpreemptive task for the entire system
Cloud VM placement by Nova Scheduler
Multithreading and context switching for concurrent application execution
questions.explanation: RIOT scheduling enables concurrent threads on constrained IoT devices.

questions.prompt: In this lecture, what is the difference between security and protection?
choices: Security is the overall goal of preventing unauthorized reading or tampering; protection defines allowed operations on objects.
Security only means encrypting passwords; protection only means antivirus software.
Security is about CPU scheduling; protection is about file compression.
Security and protection are exactly the same concept with no distinction.
questions.explanation: Security is the broad problem; protection domains specify concrete rights.

questions.prompt: Which CIA property keeps secret data from unauthorized disclosure?
questions.explanation: Confidentiality is about keeping information secret.

questions.prompt: Which CIA property prevents unauthorized modification, addition, or removal of data?
questions.explanation: Integrity means data remains correct and is changed only by authorized actions.

questions.prompt: Which CIA property is threatened by denial-of-service attacks?
questions.explanation: Availability means authorized users can use the system when needed.

questions.prompt: What does economy of mechanism require?
choices: Keep the security design simple and small to reduce the attack surface.
Hide the design so no one can inspect it.
Give every user the same full access rights.
Skip checks after the first successful access.
questions.explanation: Simple mechanisms are easier to analyze and less likely to contain security flaws.

questions.prompt: What does fail-safe defaults mean?
choices: Allow all access until an attack is detected.
Deny access by default unless explicit permission grants it.
Permit users to choose their own security policy for every object.
Trust any process that has run before.
questions.explanation: Fail-safe defaults make the safe decision be denial, not permission.

questions.prompt: What does complete mediation require?
choices: Only the first access to a file needs to be checked.
Every access to every resource must be checked against the security policy.
Checks are needed only for administrator users.
questions.explanation: Complete mediation prevents bypassing protection by reusing old unchecked access.

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of open design?
choices: All private keys must be published.
Every user should be able to change the kernel.
Access should be open to all users by default.
Security should not depend on keeping the design secret.
questions.explanation: Open design follows Kerckhoffs principle: the mechanism can be known while keys remain secret.

questions.prompt: Which principle is shown by requiring two separate keys before opening a protected object?
questions.explanation: Separation of privilege reduces risk by requiring more than one condition for access.

questions.prompt: What does least privilege require?
choices: Every program should run as administrator.
Users and programs should run with only the rights needed for their task.
Users should share one common account.
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits damage if a program or account is compromised.

questions.prompt: What does least common mechanism try to minimize?
choices: The number of cryptographic keys in the system.
The size of every user file.
Shared mechanisms depended on by multiple users.
The number of page faults during execution.
questions.explanation: Shared mechanisms can become channels for interference or leakage.

questions.prompt: Why is psychological acceptability important?
choices: Users should never know security rules exist.
Security should be difficult so only experts can log in.
Passwords should be impossible to remember.
Security mechanisms must be understandable and usable so people actually follow them.
questions.explanation: If security is too hard to use, users work around it.

questions.prompt: What is the trusted computing base?
choices: All hardware and software essential for enforcing the system security rules.
Only the network cable and router.
questions.explanation: The TCB is the part that must be trusted for security enforcement.

questions.prompt: What is the reference monitor?
choices: A performance graph for CPU usage.
A TCB component that mediates security-related system calls and cannot be bypassed.
questions.explanation: The reference monitor checks access requests against security policy.

questions.prompt: Why can a microkernel reduce security risk compared with a monolithic kernel?
choices: It removes all need for access checks.
It makes every driver run with full kernel privileges.
It can make the trusted computing base smaller.
questions.explanation: A smaller TCB gives less privileged code that must be trusted.

questions.prompt: What is a protection domain?
choices: A disk partition used only for swap space.
A list of CPU scheduling priorities.
A set of object-rights pairs describing what a process may do.
questions.explanation: A domain maps objects to allowed rights such as read, write, or execute.

questions.prompt: In UNIX-like systems, what commonly helps determine a process protection domain?
questions.explanation: User and group identities affect permissions and access rights.

questions.prompt: In a protection matrix, what do rows and columns represent?
choices: Rows are files and columns are CPU registers.
Rows are domains and columns are objects.
Rows are network packets and columns are passwords.
Rows are cache lines and columns are interrupts.
questions.explanation: Each cell lists the rights a domain has over an object.

questions.prompt: Why is a protection matrix usually implemented using ACLs or capabilities?
choices: The matrix has no rows or columns.
ACLs and capabilities remove all need for rights.
The matrix is usually sparse, so a full table wastes space.
The matrix can only store encrypted data.
questions.explanation: Sparse matrices are better represented by per-object or per-domain lists.

questions.prompt: What is an access control list?
choices: A list attached to a process containing all CPU cores.
A table that stores only encrypted passwords.
A scheduler queue for real-time tasks.
An ordered list attached to an object that names authorized domains and rights.
questions.explanation: ACLs store who can access an object and what operations are allowed.

questions.prompt: What happens in an ACL system when a user is not listed and no wildcard rule grants access?
choices: The user becomes the owner automatically.
questions.explanation: This follows fail-safe defaults.

questions.prompt: Why are groups and roles useful with ACLs?
choices: They remove the need to authenticate users.
They simplify permission management for users with similar responsibilities.
They are used only for CPU scheduling.
questions.explanation: Groups and roles avoid repeating identical permissions for many users.

questions.prompt: What is a capability?
choices: A password hash stored only in an ACL.
A ticket held by a process that grants rights to a specific object.
questions.explanation: Capabilities are associated with domains and allow efficient access checks.

questions.prompt: Why are capabilities efficient for access checks?
choices: They require scanning every file on disk.
The process refers to objects through its capability list, so searching an ACL may be avoided.
questions.explanation: A capability directly carries the authority needed for the object.

questions.prompt: How do cryptographically protected capabilities prevent fabrication?
choices: They include a value derived from a secret check field and a one-way function.
They store the password in plaintext inside the capability.
They let users choose their own check field.
They depend only on hiding the object name.
questions.explanation: Attackers cannot forge the protected field without the secret check value.

questions.prompt: What is discretionary access control?
choices: The system enforces fixed organization-wide rules that users cannot change.
The owner or user can decide who may access their files.
Only cryptographic keys decide process scheduling.
questions.explanation: DAC lets users delegate access at their discretion.

questions.prompt: What is mandatory access control?
choices: Each file owner freely chooses all access rules.
The system enforces organization-wide information-flow rules that users cannot change.
Access is based only on file extensions.
questions.explanation: MAC is needed where information flow must follow central policy.

questions.prompt: What is the Bell-LaPadula model mainly designed to protect?
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula prevents secret information from leaking downward.

questions.prompt: Which Bell-LaPadula rule is called simple security?
questions.explanation: A subject cannot read data above its clearance.

questions.prompt: Which Bell-LaPadula star property prevents leaking secrets to lower levels?
questions.explanation: No write down stops high-level information from being written to lower-level objects.

questions.prompt: What is the Biba model mainly designed to protect?
questions.explanation: Biba prevents low-integrity information from corrupting high-integrity data.
