questions.prompt: Which Biba rule prevents a low-integrity process from corrupting high-integrity data?
questions.explanation: No write up protects higher-integrity objects from lower-integrity subjects.

questions.prompt: In symmetric cryptography, how are encryption and decryption keys related?
choices: The same shared secret key is used for both.
The public key encrypts and the private key decrypts.
A new hardware interrupt is required for every byte.
questions.explanation: Secret-key cryptography uses one shared key.

questions.prompt: In public-key cryptography, which key is normally used to encrypt for one receiver?
choices: A shared one-time pad stored in the kernel
questions.explanation: Only the matching private key should decrypt data encrypted with the receiver public key.

questions.prompt: Why is public-key cryptography not usually used for all bulk data encryption?
choices: It is much slower than symmetric cryptography.
It works only on plaintext shorter than one byte.
questions.explanation: Public-key methods are powerful but expensive.

questions.prompt: How is a digital signature created in the lecture description?
choices: Encrypt the whole document with the receiver public key only.
Store the password beside the document.
Copy the document into an ACL.
Hash the document and encrypt the hash with the owner private key.
questions.explanation: The signature binds the document hash to the signer private key.

questions.prompt: How does a receiver verify a digital signature?
choices: Decrypt the signature with the signer public key and compare the hash with the document hash.
Ask the signer for their password.
Check whether the file is compressed.
questions.explanation: Matching hashes show integrity and signer authenticity.

questions.prompt: Why should login systems avoid saying whether the username was valid before checking the password?
choices: It turns passwords into one-time passwords.
questions.explanation: Different error messages can reveal valid account names.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of salt in password hashing?
choices: Encrypt the user name with the public key.
Allow all users to share one password hash.
Make precomputed password-hash tables ineffective by adding a per-password random value.
Store passwords in plaintext but hidden format.
questions.explanation: Salt forces attackers to redo work for each salt value.

questions.prompt: What is Lamport one-time password scheme based on?
choices: A reusable password sent every time.
A public ACL entry for each login.
A stack canary placed beside the password.
A chain of values generated by a one-way hash function and used in reverse order.
questions.explanation: Knowing one used password should not let the attacker compute the next required one.

questions.prompt: What is a stack canary?
choices: A secret value placed before the return address and checked before returning.
A public key stored in the file system.
A page table entry for a swapped page.
A cache line used by Flush and Reload.
questions.explanation: If a buffer overflow overwrites the return address, it likely changes the canary.

questions.prompt: What does Data Execution Prevention or NX/WX enforce?
choices: All stack pages must be executable.
Writable data pages should not be executable, and executable code pages should not be writable.
All code pages must be writable by user input.
Every file must be encrypted before execution.
questions.explanation: NX/WX blocks direct execution of injected shellcode on the stack or heap.

questions.prompt: How does return-oriented programming bypass NX protections?
choices: It makes the stack executable again by default.
It guesses the password hash directly.
It chains existing code gadgets ending in return instructions.
It disables all cache side channels.
questions.explanation: ROP reuses executable code already present in memory.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of ASLR?
choices: Force all code to run in kernel mode.
Remove all dynamic libraries from memory.
Randomize memory locations so attackers cannot reliably know addresses of buffers or gadgets.
questions.explanation: ASLR makes address-dependent exploits harder.

questions.prompt: What can weaken ASLR?
choices: A memory disclosure vulnerability that leaks addresses.
questions.explanation: If addresses leak, randomization can be calculated or bypassed.

questions.prompt: When does a format string vulnerability occur?
choices: When a process has too many file descriptors.
When user-controlled input is passed directly as the printf format string.
When a disk block is allocated twice.
When the kernel uses a page cache.
questions.explanation: Attackers can use format specifiers to read or write memory.

questions.prompt: Which format specifier can be abused to write a value in a format string attack?
questions.explanation: %n writes the number of printed characters to a supplied address.

questions.prompt: What is a use-after-free vulnerability?
choices: Freeing memory only after the program exits.
Reading a file after closing the terminal.
Using a process after it has been scheduled.
Using a pointer after the memory it references has been freed.
questions.explanation: Freed memory may be reallocated with attacker-controlled content.

questions.prompt: What is type confusion?
choices: Treating an object as the wrong type, such as casting a parent object to the wrong child class.
Using the wrong password salt length.
Confusing TCP with UDP in a routing table.
Changing a file extension after encryption.
questions.explanation: Type confusion can call methods or access fields not valid for the real object.

questions.prompt: Why can a null pointer dereference become dangerous in some kernels?
choices: A null pointer always encrypts memory automatically.
If user and kernel share an address space, attacker code mapped at address zero may run with kernel privileges.
It prevents all system calls from returning.
It only causes slower disk access.
questions.explanation: Dereferencing NULL in privileged code can jump into attacker-controlled memory in vulnerable designs.

questions.prompt: Why can integer overflow lead to buffer overflow?
choices: Integers are stored only in the file system.
A wrapped integer may cause too-small allocation for a large amount of data.
questions.explanation: Silent wraparound can make size calculations wrong.

questions.prompt: What is command injection?
choices: Injecting a cache line into RAM.
Adding a user to an ACL group.
Compiling commands into a static library.
Tricking a program into executing shell commands through crafted input.
questions.explanation: Special characters can change the command a program sends to the shell.

questions.prompt: What does TOCTOU mean?
choices: The object checked for permission may be changed before it is used.
The password is checked twice before login.
Two CPUs execute the same instruction at once.
A file is encrypted before hashing.
questions.explanation: Time Of Check To Time Of Use attacks exploit a race between checking and acting.

questions.prompt: How is a covert channel different from normal authorized communication?
choices: It is a faster version of TCP.
It is sneaky communication between processes not authorized to talk.
It is any public API call.
It is only used to compress files.
questions.explanation: Covert channels bypass intended communication restrictions.

questions.prompt: How is a side channel different from a covert channel?
choices: A side channel requires both processes to intentionally communicate.
A side channel is just an ACL entry.
A side channel leaks information without the victim cooperating.
A side channel is a password salt.
questions.explanation: Side channels exploit observable effects such as cache timing.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of Flush and Reload?
choices: Encrypt every cache line with RSA.
Flush shared cache lines, wait for victim execution, reload and time access to infer use.
questions.explanation: Fast reload indicates the victim accessed that cache line.

questions.prompt: What are transient instructions?
choices: Instructions executed speculatively or out of order but later squashed architecturally.
Instructions stored permanently in the file system journal.
Instructions that never reach the CPU pipeline.
Instructions used only by boot loaders.
questions.explanation: Transient execution can leave microarchitectural traces even when results are undone.

questions.prompt: What does Meltdown exploit?
choices: A branch predictor trained to skip bounds checks only.
Transient access to kernel memory by unprivileged code before a fault is raised.
A password hash without salt only.
questions.explanation: Meltdown leaks privileged data through cache side effects after transient execution.

questions.prompt: What does Spectre exploit?
choices: A missing stack canary in a boot loader only.
Mistraining branch prediction so speculative execution accesses data that should be blocked.
questions.explanation: Spectre tricks speculation past security checks and leaks through side channels.

questions.prompt: Which mitigation separates kernel and user address spaces to stop Meltdown?
questions.explanation: Kernel Page Table Isolation reduces the mapped kernel data visible during user execution.

questions.prompt: What is the cost of fences and other transient-execution mitigations?
choices: They remove all need for passwords.
They prevent programs from using files.
questions.explanation: Stopping speculation across sensitive points can slow execution.

questions.prompt: What is a logic bomb?
choices: Malicious code that triggers under specific conditions.
questions.explanation: A logic bomb may activate when a condition such as an employee ID missing from payroll occurs.

questions.prompt: What is a back door?
choices: A normal ACL rule visible to all users.
A scheduler queue for background jobs.
A secret entry point that bypasses normal authentication.
questions.explanation: Back doors allow hidden access outside regular controls.

questions.prompt: What is login spoofing?
choices: A page fault during program loading.
A fake login screen collects credentials and then exits to the real login screen.
questions.explanation: Login spoofing tricks users into giving credentials to an attacker.

questions.prompt: Why is fine-grained randomization stronger than coarse KASLR?
choices: It randomizes smaller items such as functions, heap objects, and stack variables.
It moves the whole kernel as one fixed segment only.
It disables all randomization after boot.
It stores addresses in plaintext logs.
questions.explanation: If one pointer leaks, coarse randomization may be easier to bypass.

questions.prompt: What does control-flow integrity enforce?
choices: Every file must be signed before reading.
Indirect jumps and returns must land on legitimate labeled targets.
All pages must be writable and executable.
Passwords must be changed every minute.
questions.explanation: CFI restricts control transfers to valid program paths.

questions.prompt: What does secure boot verify?
choices: Only the desktop wallpaper before login.
Only the user password after the kernel starts.
Each boot stage verifies the digital signature of the next stage before running it.
Only network packets after boot completes.
questions.explanation: Secure boot builds a chain of trust from firmware to bootloader to kernel.

questions.prompt: What does a TPM record for remote attestation?
choices: Every keystroke typed by the user.
Only file names in the current directory.
The scheduling quantum of each process.
Hashes of loaded code and data in platform configuration registers.
questions.explanation: Signed PCR values can prove to a remote party how the system booted.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of sandboxing untrusted code?
choices: Restrict code and data references to assigned memory ranges or allowed resources.
Give foreign code full kernel privileges.
questions.explanation: Sandboxing limits damage from untrusted code.

exams.title: Lecture 11 - Linux OS
exams.description: Manual questions from lecture 11 slides.md, excluding the out-of-exam slide range.

questions.prompt: What was a major original design goal of Linux?
choices: UNIX compatibility in a free, open-source kernel.
Replacing all system libraries with firmware.
questions.explanation: Linux was developed by Linus Torvalds with UNIX compatibility as a central goal.

questions.prompt: What limitation did Linux version 0.01 have?
choices: It supported all modern architectures and SMP.
It had no networking and ran only on 80386-compatible PC hardware.
It included full journaling and CFS.
It ran only as an Android app.
questions.explanation: The earliest kernel was small and had very limited driver and file-system support.

questions.prompt: Which feature was added in Linux 1.0?
questions.explanation: Linux 1.0 added important networking and hardware support.

questions.prompt: What two major capabilities did Linux 2.0 add?
choices: Only a new desktop theme and browser.
Mandatory cloud deployment and object storage.
questions.explanation: Linux 2.0 expanded portability and SMP support.

questions.prompt: What did later Linux 2.4 and 2.6 kernels improve?
choices: SMP support, journaling file systems, preemptive kernel behavior, and 64-bit memory support.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these as important later Linux kernel improvements.

questions.prompt: Which projects contributed many tools and libraries to the Linux system?
choices: BSD, MIT X Window System, and GNU.
questions.explanation: Linux combines its original kernel with many UNIX-compatible tools.

questions.prompt: What does the File System Hierarchy Standard specify?
choices: Which cryptographic key length to use.
Where configuration files, libraries, binaries, and run-time data should be stored.
Which packet should be routed first.
questions.explanation: FHS helps distributions remain compatible in file-system layout.

questions.prompt: What is a Linux distribution?
choices: Only a single kernel object file.
A precompiled set of packages including the kernel, utilities, management tools, and applications.
questions.explanation: Distributions package Linux into installable systems.

questions.prompt: Why is RPM important across Linux distributions?
choices: It provides a package file format that can permit compatibility among distributions.
It is a CPU scheduling algorithm.
It is a network device driver.
questions.explanation: RPM is a package format used for software distribution.

questions.prompt: What does the GNU GPL require for Linux derivatives?
choices: All Linux systems must be sold for free with no services.
They cannot be made proprietary as binary-only products without source availability.
Source code must be hidden from users.
Only Microsoft can redistribute the kernel.
questions.explanation: GPL allows selling distributions but requires source-code rights.

questions.prompt: Which design goals are emphasized for Linux?
choices: Only graphical animation and game performance.
No networking and no multiuser support.
Speed, efficiency, standardization, and UNIX-compatible behavior.
questions.explanation: Linux aims to be a multiuser, multitasking UNIX-compatible system.

questions.prompt: Which three bodies of code form a typical Linux system?
choices: Kernel, system libraries, and system utilities.
questions.explanation: The kernel is privileged; libraries and utilities provide user-level OS functionality.

questions.prompt: What does the Linux kernel maintain?
choices: The key operating-system abstractions and privileged access to hardware resources.
questions.explanation: Kernel code runs in kernel mode and manages resources.

questions.prompt: What are Linux kernel modules?
choices: Kernel code sections that can be loaded and unloaded independently.
questions.explanation: Modules commonly implement drivers, file systems, or network protocols.

questions.prompt: Why are kernel modules useful?
choices: They require every driver to be compiled into the kernel permanently.
They prevent third parties from writing drivers.
They allow a minimal standard kernel with extra drivers or file systems loaded as needed.
They remove the need for device registration.
questions.explanation: Modules make the kernel more flexible and configurable.

questions.prompt: What does module management handle?
choices: Loading modules into memory and resolving symbols they may reference.
questions.explanation: Module management connects loaded modules with kernel symbols and memory.

questions.prompt: What is the module requestor responsible for?
choices: Loading requested but currently unloaded modules and unloading unused dynamic modules.
questions.explanation: The requestor automates module loading and cleanup.

questions.prompt: What does driver registration allow?
choices: Users can register file names as passwords.
Modules can tell the kernel that a new driver, file system, protocol, or binary format is available.
A scheduler can remove all priorities.
questions.explanation: Registration updates dynamic kernel tables.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of Linux conflict resolution for drivers?
choices: Force all drivers to use the same interrupt at once.
Prevent drivers from clashing over hardware resources or interfering with existing drivers.
Convert block devices into network protocols.
questions.explanation: The kernel tracks allocated hardware resources.

questions.prompt: How does UNIX process management separate process creation and program execution?
choices: exec creates a process, and fork deletes it.
clone always replaces both fork and exec with one operation.
fork creates a process, and exec runs a new program in it.
questions.explanation: UNIX separates creating a process from loading a new program.

questions.prompt: What is a PID?
choices: A unique process identifier used by the OS to refer to a process.
A page-table entry for a disk block.
questions.explanation: System calls use PIDs to signal, wait for, or modify processes.

questions.prompt: What do process credentials determine?
choices: The page size used by the processor.
The process rights to access system resources and files.
questions.explanation: User IDs and group IDs are used in access control.

questions.prompt: What is a Linux process personality?
choices: A text label shown in the desktop taskbar only.
An identifier that can slightly modify system-call semantics for compatibility.
A CPU register saved during interrupts only.
questions.explanation: Personality helps emulation libraries mimic different UNIX variants.

questions.prompt: What does a process namespace provide?
choices: A list of all CPU time slices.
A table of all TCP ports.
A specific view of the file-system hierarchy, possibly with its own root and mounts.
questions.explanation: Namespaces can isolate file-system views.

questions.prompt: What is contained in a process environment?
choices: Argument and environment vectors inherited from the parent.
questions.explanation: Environment variables customize behavior per process.

questions.prompt: What is the scheduling context of a Linux process?
choices: The list of all mounted file systems.
Information the scheduler needs to suspend and restart the process.
questions.explanation: Scheduling context is the most important part of the changing process context.

questions.prompt: How does a process refer to open files in Linux?
choices: By indexes into its file table called file descriptors.
questions.explanation: A file descriptor is an index into the process open-file table.

questions.prompt: What is stored in a process file-system context?
choices: The current root and default directories used for opening new files.
questions.explanation: The file-system context affects path resolution for new opens.

questions.prompt: What does the virtual-memory context describe?
choices: The contents of the process private address space.
questions.explanation: Virtual-memory context tracks the address space.

questions.prompt: How does Linux internally represent threads?
choices: Threads are not represented in the kernel at all.
A thread is a task that may share address-space structures with its parent.
Threads are always separate physical CPUs.
Threads are only entries in the file system.
questions.explanation: Linux uses tasks for both processes and threads.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between fork and clone in Linux?
choices: fork shares everything and clone shares nothing.
fork creates a task with a new context; clone can share selected parent data structures.
fork is used only for device drivers.
questions.explanation: clone gives fine-grained control over sharing between threads.

questions.prompt: What does Linux scheduling allocate?
choices: CPU time to user tasks and kernel tasks.
questions.explanation: Linux scheduling includes processes and kernel work.

questions.prompt: What problem did the old O(1) scheduler have?
choices: It had challenges with interactive performance.
It could not schedule real-time priorities at all.
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that O(1) was preemptive and priority-based but had interactive issues.

questions.prompt: What is a key idea of the Completely Fair Scheduler?
choices: It always runs the newest process first.
It allocates each task a fair portion of processor time instead of using a fixed traditional time slice.
It gives every task exactly one second regardless of load.
questions.explanation: CFS models fair sharing of CPU time.

questions.prompt: How does a smaller nice value affect CFS?
choices: It removes the task from scheduling.
It makes the task use only disk I/O.
It gives the task a higher weight and priority.
It disables the task address space.
questions.explanation: CFS weights runnable tasks using nice values.

questions.prompt: What is target latency in CFS?
choices: The maximum time to load a kernel module.
The time needed for password hashing.
The desired interval during which each runnable task should run at least once.
questions.explanation: Target latency influences how CFS divides CPU time among runnable tasks.

questions.prompt: Why does CFS use minimum granularity?
choices: To ensure each run has a reasonable amount of CPU time.
To make every task run for zero time.
To replace all process priorities with file permissions.
questions.explanation: Minimum granularity avoids impractically tiny slices.

questions.prompt: What are the two main ways execution enters kernel mode?
choices: A system service request or a hardware interrupt.
questions.explanation: Kernel code runs for system calls, faults, and device interrupts.

questions.prompt: Why does kernel synchronization matter?
choices: All kernel code should run without locks on SMP.
It is only about formatting file names.
Kernel critical sections must be protected from concurrent access by other critical sections.
It prevents users from reading documentation.
questions.explanation: Shared kernel data structures need mutual exclusion.

questions.prompt: How did normal Linux kernel code traditionally avoid preemption in critical sections?
choices: It ran every driver in a browser.
Kernel code was nonpreemptible until returning toward user mode.
questions.explanation: Nonpreemptibility helped protect kernel critical sections.

questions.prompt: How are interrupt-service critical sections protected?
choices: By using interrupt control hardware to disable interrupts during critical sections.
questions.explanation: Disabling interrupts prevents interrupt handlers from entering conflicting sections.

questions.prompt: Why does Linux split interrupt work into top halves and bottom halves?
choices: To keep urgent interrupt handling short while deferring longer work with interrupts enabled.
To force every process to wait for disk I/O.
To replace threads with virtual memory regions.
questions.explanation: Top halves handle immediate interrupt work; bottom halves do deferred work.

questions.prompt: What is an interrupt protection level?
choices: A level that can be interrupted only by code running at a higher level.
questions.explanation: Lower or equal levels cannot interrupt the current level.

questions.prompt: What did early Linux SMP use to protect nonpreemptible kernel execution?
choices: One page table per device driver.
A separate disk partition for each CPU.
A single kernel spinlock allowing only one CPU in kernel mode at a time.
questions.explanation: The single spinlock was simple but limited scalability.

questions.prompt: How did later Linux versions improve SMP scalability?
choices: By splitting the single spinlock into finer-grained locks and adding affinity and load balancing.
By disabling all but one processor.
By using only user-space file locks.
questions.explanation: Fine-grained locking allows more parallel kernel execution.

questions.prompt: What does Linux physical memory management allocate and free?
choices: Pages, groups of pages, and small blocks of memory.
questions.explanation: The physical memory system manages page-level and small kernel allocations.

questions.prompt: How does the buddy allocator work?
choices: It stores every page in a linked list sorted by file name.
It splits larger free regions into partners and recombines free partners into larger regions.
questions.explanation: Buddy allocation manages contiguous physical pages.

questions.prompt: What is the slab allocator used for?
choices: Kernel memory allocations of small objects.
questions.explanation: The slab allocator is used for efficient kernel object allocation.

questions.prompt: What is the Linux page cache?
choices: The kernel main cache for files and block-device I/O, storing pages of file contents.
A table of loaded password salts.
questions.explanation: The page cache connects file I/O and virtual memory.

questions.prompt: What two views of a process address space does the VM manager maintain?
choices: A network view and a password view.
A logical region view and a physical page-table view.
A bootloader view and a BIOS view.
A package view and a distribution view.
questions.explanation: Linux tracks address-space layout separately from hardware mappings.

questions.prompt: What can back a Linux virtual-memory region?
questions.explanation: Backing store describes where pages come from.

questions.prompt: What happens to address spaces after fork in Linux?
choices: The child receives no address space.
All pages are immediately copied byte by byte.
The parent is deleted before the child runs.
Parent and child initially share physical pages with updated reference counts.
questions.explanation: Linux uses sharing and copy-on-write style behavior after fork.

questions.prompt: What does the pageout-policy algorithm decide?
choices: Which pages to write to disk and when.
Which user may open a file.
questions.explanation: Pageout policy is part of the paging system.

questions.prompt: What is the Linux kernel virtual-memory area?
choices: A reserved region in every process address space used by the kernel.
questions.explanation: The kernel reserves part of virtual address space for internal use.

questions.prompt: How does Linux support multiple executable formats?
choices: It stores every program as text only.
It requires all binaries to be ELF only with no loader table.
It keeps a table of loader functions tried during exec.
It runs all binaries through the shell.
questions.explanation: Loader registration lets Linux support formats such as ELF and a.out.

questions.prompt: Why is dynamic linking more efficient than static linking?
choices: Every executable contains all library code.
It prevents programs from using shared libraries.
Common libraries can be loaded once and shared, saving memory and disk space.
questions.explanation: Dynamic linking avoids duplicate copies of common library routines.

questions.prompt: What is position-independent code used for in shared libraries?
choices: It lets shared libraries be loaded at different virtual-memory locations.
It prevents programs from using virtual memory.
It converts libraries into kernel modules.
questions.explanation: PIC supports flexible library placement.

questions.prompt: What does the Linux VFS provide?
choices: A single physical disk layout required by all file systems.
An abstraction layer hiding details of different file-system implementations.
A CPU scheduler for kernel threads.
questions.explanation: VFS lets Linux support many file systems through common objects and operations.

questions.prompt: Which objects are central in the Linux VFS?
choices: PID, UID, GID, and nice objects only.
TCP, UDP, ICMP, and IP objects only.
inode, file, superblock, and dentry objects.
Stack, heap, code, and data objects only.
questions.explanation: These VFS objects model files, open files, file systems, and directory entries.

questions.prompt: What is ext3?
choices: A standard on-disk Linux file system that superseded ext2 and supports journaling.
questions.explanation: ext3 was a common Linux disk file system.

questions.prompt: How does ext3 improve allocation locality?
choices: It uses cluster allocation policies to place logically adjacent blocks physically close together.
It scatters every block randomly across the disk.
It stores all files only in one block.
questions.explanation: Adjacent physical placement can improve I/O performance.

questions.prompt: What does ext3 journaling do?
choices: Encrypts every file with a public key.
Writes file-system updates to a log as transactions before applying them to the file system.
Copies all user data to the network.
questions.explanation: Journaling speeds recovery after crashes.

questions.prompt: What does the proc file system store on disk?
choices: It stores all user documents permanently.
It stores no real data; contents are computed on demand.
questions.explanation: proc presents kernel and process information as generated files.

questions.prompt: Which two caches does Linux use for disk-oriented I/O?
choices: DNS cache and ARP cache only.
Page cache for data and buffer cache for metadata.
questions.explanation: Linux caches file data and disk metadata separately in this description.

questions.prompt: What is a block device?
choices: A device allowing random access to independent fixed-size blocks.
A device that never supports random access.
questions.explanation: Disks are typical block devices.

questions.prompt: What is a character device?
choices: A device driver that does not offer random access to fixed-size blocks.
questions.explanation: Character devices pass reads and writes mostly directly to the driver.

questions.prompt: What does terminal line discipline do?
choices: It interprets terminal data and connects it to process standard input and output.
questions.explanation: The tty line discipline manages terminal interaction with processes.

questions.prompt: How does Linux inform processes that an event occurred?
questions.explanation: Signals notify processes of events but carry limited information.

questions.prompt: Why can System V semaphores be useful for IPC?
choices: They can scale better and support atomic operations on multiple semaphores.
They can carry large files directly.
They replace all pipes and shared memory.
questions.explanation: Semaphores coordinate processes and can support atomic multi-semaphore operations.

questions.prompt: What does a pipe provide?
choices: A shared virtual address space for all processes.
A communication channel where data written to one end can be read from the other.
questions.explanation: Pipes pass streams of data between related processes.

questions.prompt: Why must shared memory be combined with another IPC mechanism?
choices: It cannot be read by more than one process.
It is slower than all other IPC methods.
It needs synchronization so processes do not race on shared data.
questions.explanation: Shared memory is fast but does not automatically coordinate access.

questions.prompt: What are the three internal Linux networking layers listed in the lecture?
choices: Socket interface, protocol drivers, and network device drivers.
questions.explanation: Linux networking is layered from sockets to protocols to devices.

questions.prompt: Which protocols sit above IP routing in the Internet protocol suite?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists UDP, TCP, and ICMP above routing.

questions.prompt: What is PAM in Linux?
choices: A pluggable authentication system based on a shared library.
questions.explanation: PAM lets system components use common authentication modules.

questions.prompt: How does traditional UNIX access control identify users and groups?
choices: With numeric uid and gid values.
questions.explanation: Linux uses uid and gid for ownership and permission checks.

questions.prompt: What does a UNIX protection mask specify?
choices: Only CPU priority and target latency.
Read, write, and execute permissions for owner, group, and world.
questions.explanation: Protection masks define standard UNIX file access modes.

questions.prompt: What does saved user ID allow?
choices: A process can drop and later reacquire its effective uid.
A process can bypass all permissions permanently.
A process can become a kernel module.
A file system can ignore owners.
questions.explanation: Saved uid supports safer privilege changes.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of Linux capabilities as a process characteristic?
choices: Give every process all root rights.
Grant only a subset of traditional effective-uid privileges.
questions.explanation: Capabilities split root-like privilege into smaller rights.

exams.title: Lecture 12 - Windows 10
exams.description: Manual questions from lecture 12 slides.md, excluding the out-of-exam slide range.

questions.prompt: Why did Microsoft start developing Windows NT?
choices: To build a portable new technology OS supporting OS/2 and POSIX APIs.
To create a small embedded sensor OS only.
To replace all APIs with MS-DOS interrupts only.
questions.explanation: NT began as a portable OS project and later adopted Win32 as native.

questions.prompt: Why did NT switch toward the Win32 API as native?
choices: Because POSIX was removed from computing entirely.
Because Windows 3.0 made Win32-style Windows applications important.
Because OS/2 became the only Windows API.
Because NT ran only on smartphones.
questions.explanation: The lecture notes NT changed direction during development.

questions.prompt: Which description fits Windows 10 in the lecture?
choices: A microcontroller firmware with no GUI.
A 32-bit and 64-bit preemptive multitasking OS for Intel microprocessors.
A cloud-only operating system with no local processes.
questions.explanation: Windows 10 supports preemptive multitasking and both 32-bit and 64-bit execution.

questions.prompt: Which list contains major Windows 10 goals?
choices: Only file compression and wallpaper management.
Only cloud object storage and service brokerage.
Portability, security, POSIX compliance, multiprocessor support, extensibility, international support, and compatibility.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these as Windows 10 goals.

questions.prompt: What architectural style does the lecture associate with Windows 10?
choices: A single user-mode shell with no kernel.
questions.explanation: Windows is described as layered and micro-kernel based.

questions.prompt: Which Windows 10 feature supports older binaries through a bridge?
questions.explanation: The lecture mentions Windows Desktop Bridge for older binaries.

questions.prompt: Which security mechanisms are part of Windows 10 design principles?
choices: Only username/password with no exploit mitigations.
ACLs, integrity levels, encryption, ASLR, DEP, CFG, ACG, and digital signatures.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these Windows security mechanisms.

questions.prompt: What do Device Guard policies help control?
choices: Which process gets a shorter time slice.
Which file uses a smaller cluster size.
Which DNS server resolves a name.
Which signed code is allowed to run on the system.
questions.explanation: Device Guard can restrict allowed signers.

questions.prompt: What extensibility mechanisms are listed for Windows 10?
questions.explanation: Windows uses remote and advanced local procedure calls.

questions.prompt: How does Windows 10 support portability?
choices: All code is written in assembly for one CPU.
Most code is C/C++, processor-specific code is small, and HAL isolates platform-dependent code.
It removes the HAL and embeds hardware assumptions everywhere.
It runs only on one exact motherboard.
questions.explanation: The HAL DLL hides platform differences.

questions.prompt: Which design principle uses hardware protection for virtual memory and software protection for OS resources?
questions.explanation: The lecture ties reliability to hardware and software protection mechanisms.

questions.prompt: How does Windows 10 support international use?
choices: Through the national language support API.
questions.explanation: NLS supports different locales.

questions.prompt: Which feature helps Windows energy efficiency on portable devices?
choices: Dynamic tick, process lifetime management, desktop activity moderator, and connected standby.
Forcing all CPUs to run at maximum speed.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these energy-saving features.

questions.prompt: Which components run in protected mode in Windows architecture?
questions.explanation: Protected mode contains the privileged OS layers.

questions.prompt: What runs in user mode in the Windows architecture?
choices: A collection of subsystems, including environmental and protection subsystems.
questions.explanation: User-mode subsystems provide operating environments and services.

questions.prompt: What do Virtual Trust Levels provide?
choices: A replacement for all process priorities.
A normal world and secure world boundary implemented with Hyper-V virtualization.
questions.explanation: VTL 0 and VTL 1 separate normal and secure execution worlds.

questions.prompt: What is in the secure world of VSM?
choices: A secure kernel, secure executive, and trustlets.
questions.explanation: VSM uses isolated secure components.

questions.prompt: What are four main Windows kernel responsibilities?
choices: Only registry editing and file compression.
Thread scheduling, interrupt and exception handling, low-level processor synchronization, and power-failure recovery.
questions.explanation: The kernel is the foundation for executive and subsystems.

questions.prompt: Which Windows kernel objects control dispatching and synchronization?
choices: Dispatcher objects such as events, mutexes, semaphores, threads, and timers.
questions.explanation: Dispatcher objects support scheduling and synchronization.

questions.prompt: What are Windows control objects?
choices: Objects such as APCs, interrupts, power notify, power status, process, and profile objects.
questions.explanation: Control objects represent lower-level kernel control structures.

questions.prompt: What is the unit of execution scheduled by the Windows kernel dispatcher?
questions.explanation: Processes own resources, but threads are scheduled.

questions.prompt: What does a Windows process contain?
choices: A virtual address space, base-priority information, and processor affinity.
questions.explanation: A process is a container for resources and execution context.

questions.prompt: Which pair of execution modes and stacks can a Windows thread have?
choices: Only user mode with no kernel stack.
Only secure mode with no user stack.
User mode and kernel mode, with one stack for each.
Only BIOS mode and POST mode.
questions.explanation: Trap handling switches mode and stack when needed.

questions.prompt: Which Windows priority ranges are real-time and variable?
questions.explanation: Windows uses a 32-level priority scheme.

questions.prompt: What kind of threads does Windows priority strategy favor for good response?
questions.explanation: The strategy keeps the GUI responsive and I/O devices busy.

questions.prompt: When can Windows scheduling occur?
choices: When a thread becomes ready or waiting, terminates, or when priority or affinity changes.
Only when a file is compressed.
questions.explanation: These events can cause dispatcher decisions.

questions.prompt: Why is Windows real-time scheduling described as soft real-time?
choices: All threads are guaranteed to start within one nanosecond.
Real-time threads get preference, but no exact start-time guarantee is made.
Real-time scheduling applies only to files.
questions.explanation: Windows gives preferential CPU access but not hard deadlines.

questions.prompt: What does the Windows trap handler handle?
choices: Exceptions and interrupts generated by hardware or software.
questions.explanation: Trap handling dispatches exceptions and interrupts.

questions.prompt: How does Windows achieve multiprocessor mutual exclusion in the kernel?
choices: Using spin locks in global memory.
questions.explanation: Spin locks protect shared kernel data on multiprocessors.

questions.prompt: What does the Windows object manager do?
choices: Generates handles, checks security, and tracks which processes use objects.
questions.explanation: The object manager supervises OS objects.

questions.prompt: How can a process get a handle to a Windows object?
choices: Only by editing the registry manually.
By creating it, opening it, inheriting it, or receiving a duplicated handle.
Only by loading a device driver.
questions.explanation: Handles are per-process references to objects.

questions.prompt: How are Windows executive object names structured?
choices: Like raw physical memory addresses only.
Like file path names, with symbolic links for aliases.
questions.explanation: Named objects form a namespace similar to paths.

questions.prompt: What two steps does the Windows virtual memory manager use to allocate memory?
choices: Reserve address space, then commit backing space in the paging file.
Create a process, then create a thread.
Resolve DNS, then open a socket.
questions.explanation: Reservation and commitment are distinct VM allocation steps.

questions.prompt: Which page sizes can Windows use according to hardware support in the lecture?
questions.explanation: Windows VM uses hardware-supported page sizes.

questions.prompt: Which states can a Windows page be in?
choices: Ready, running, waiting, and terminated only.
Open, closed, parsed, and deleted only.
Valid, zeroed, free, standby, modified, or bad.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists six page states.

questions.prompt: What does a page file page-table entry contain?
choices: Protection bits, page-file offset, paging-file selector, and bookkeeping bits.
questions.explanation: A page-file PTE identifies where a paged-out page is stored.

questions.prompt: What does the Windows process manager provide?
choices: Services for creating, deleting, and using processes and threads.
questions.explanation: The executive process manager handles process/thread services.

questions.prompt: How are Docker containers supported in Windows according to the lecture?
questions.explanation: Silos are job-object based container support.

questions.prompt: How does LPC handle small messages?
choices: By always using shared memory for every byte.
By sending all requests through DNS.
By using a port message queue and copying messages between processes.
By writing them to the registry.
questions.explanation: Small LPC messages up to about 256 bytes use queues and copying.

questions.prompt: How does LPC avoid copying large messages?
choices: By converting them to SMB printer messages.
By using a shared memory section object for the channel.
questions.explanation: Large LPC transfers can share memory instead of copying.

questions.prompt: What is the Windows I/O manager responsible for?
choices: File systems, cache management, device drivers, and network drivers.
questions.explanation: The I/O manager coordinates much of the Windows I/O system.

questions.prompt: What does the I/O manager do with the VM manager?
questions.explanation: Memory-mapped files connect I/O and virtual memory.

questions.prompt: What does the security reference monitor check when a process opens an object handle?
choices: The file cluster number against the CPU priority.
The DNS record against the registry hive.
The process security token against the object access control list.
The bootloader signature against a pipe.
questions.explanation: This gives uniform runtime access validation.

questions.prompt: What does the Plug-and-Play manager do?
choices: Stores file metadata in the MFT.
Resolves computer names to IP addresses.
Recognizes hardware changes, loads appropriate drivers, and tracks device resources.
questions.explanation: PnP adapts to added devices such as PCI or USB.

questions.prompt: What does the Windows power manager control?
choices: Sleep, hibernation, processor power behavior, and device power states.
questions.explanation: Power management handles system and device power conditions.

questions.prompt: What is the Windows registry?
choices: A file-system journal for user data only.
Configuration repositories called hives managed by the configuration manager.
questions.explanation: The registry stores system, user, software, security, and boot configuration.

questions.prompt: What does UEFI Secure Boot verify during boot?
choices: Digital signatures of firmware and boot-time components.
questions.explanation: Secure Boot helps ensure trusted boot components.

questions.prompt: What happens if Windows is resuming from hibernation?
choices: All users are forced into POSIX subsystem.
questions.explanation: winresume.efi restores hibernated state.

questions.prompt: Which process is started during kernel initialization along with idle and system processes?
questions.explanation: SMSS is one of the early Windows processes.

questions.prompt: What is the main Windows operating environment subsystem?
questions.explanation: Win32 starts processes and provides keyboard, mouse, and graphical display capabilities.

questions.prompt: What provides the MS-DOS environment in Windows?
choices: A virtual DOS machine (VDM) user-mode process.
questions.explanation: VDM emulates MS-DOS behavior in user mode.

questions.prompt: What does the POSIX subsystem support?
choices: Applications following the POSIX.1 UNIX model.
questions.explanation: The POSIX subsystem lets POSIX-style applications run.

questions.prompt: What does the logon and security subsystem do?
choices: Authenticates users and works with authentication packages such as Kerberos.
questions.explanation: Windows uses account names, passwords, and Kerberos for network authentication.

questions.prompt: What is the fundamental NTFS structure?
choices: A volume based on a logical disk partition.
questions.explanation: NTFS organizes storage into volumes.

questions.prompt: What is the NTFS allocation unit?
choices: A cluster made from a power-of-two number of disk sectors.
questions.explanation: Clusters are the basic disk allocation units in NTFS.

questions.prompt: What is the Master File Table?
choices: A scheduling table for real-time priorities.
A list of loaded dynamic libraries.
A special file containing records that describe every file on an NTFS volume.
questions.explanation: NTFS metadata is stored in regular files, including the MFT.

questions.prompt: What is an NTFS file reference?
choices: A 64-bit value with a 48-bit file number and 16-bit sequence number.
questions.explanation: File references uniquely identify files and support consistency checks.

questions.prompt: What does NTFS log for recovery?
choices: Redo and undo records for transactions plus a commit record.
questions.explanation: Logged transactions let NTFS restore metadata consistency.

questions.prompt: What does NTFS recovery guarantee after a crash?
choices: Every byte of every user file is always recovered.
The registry is always rolled back.
File-system metadata structures can be made consistent, but all user file data is not guaranteed.
questions.explanation: NTFS recovery protects file-system structures more than arbitrary user data.

questions.prompt: Where does NTFS store file security information?
choices: In a security descriptor attribute in the MFT record.
questions.explanation: The descriptor includes owner token information and ACLs.

questions.prompt: How does NTFS compress files?
choices: It divides file data into compression units of 16 contiguous clusters.
It stores every file as one CPU register.
It sends files through SMB before storing them.
questions.explanation: Compression units are the NTFS compression granularity.

questions.prompt: How does NTFS represent sparse zero-filled regions?
choices: It stores every zero cluster physically.
It leaves gaps in virtual cluster numbers and zero-fills on reads.
It converts zeros into DNS names.
It prevents the file from being opened.
questions.explanation: Sparse files save disk space for all-zero regions.

questions.prompt: What does NDIS separate?
questions.explanation: NDIS lets adapters and protocols change independently.

questions.prompt: What does TDI enable?
choices: Objects can be named like paths.
Session layer components can use available transport mechanisms.
questions.explanation: The Transport Driver Interface abstracts transport use.

questions.prompt: What protocol sends I/O requests over the network in Windows file sharing?
questions.explanation: Server Message Block is used for network I/O requests.

questions.prompt: Which SMB message types are listed?
choices: Session control, file, printer, and message.
Open, close, delete, and parse only.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists four SMB message categories.

questions.prompt: What is NetBIOS used for?
choices: Logical names, sessions between names, and reliable data transfer for sessions.
NTFS redo and undo logging only.
questions.explanation: NetBIOS abstracts network naming and sessions.

questions.prompt: Which protocols connect Windows to many operating systems and hardware platforms?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP provides broad interoperability.

questions.prompt: What is PPTP used for?
choices: Communication between Remote Access Server modules over the Internet.
questions.explanation: Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol supports remote access connectivity.

questions.prompt: What are named pipes in Windows networking?
choices: Connection-oriented messaging mechanisms named by UNC.
questions.explanation: Named pipes support interprocess and network communication.

questions.prompt: What are mailslots used for?
choices: Connectionless broadcast applications such as finding network components.
questions.explanation: Mailslots are suitable for broadcast-style messages.

questions.prompt: What does Winsock provide?
choices: A standardized session-layer API over many transport protocols.
questions.explanation: Winsock hides differences among transport protocols.

questions.prompt: What standard does Windows RPC follow?
questions.explanation: Following DCE helps portability of RPC programs.

questions.prompt: What is a redirector?
choices: A client-side kernel component that forwards I/O requests to remote files.
questions.explanation: Redirectors make remote files appear local through the Windows I/O API.

questions.prompt: What is the role of MUP in remote file access?
choices: It stores every file in the MFT.
It sends the I/O request asynchronously to registered redirectors and caches which one handles the file.
questions.explanation: The Multiple UNC Provider chooses the appropriate redirector.

questions.prompt: In remote file access, what happens after the server driver receives a request?
choices: It changes the client priority class.
It creates a new registry hive.
It passes the request to the proper local file system driver and device driver.
questions.explanation: The server side satisfies the file request locally and sends results back.

questions.prompt: What is a Windows domain?
choices: A group of Windows machines sharing a common security policy and user database.
questions.explanation: Domains manage global access rights.

questions.prompt: What is a two-way transitive trust relationship?
choices: Only A trusts B and nobody else is affected.
If A trusts B and B trusts C, A, B, and C can trust each other through the chain.
Trust is limited to one local file.
questions.explanation: Transitive trust passes through the hierarchy.

questions.prompt: What is crosslink trust used for?
choices: To bypass hierarchy and reduce authentication traffic.
questions.explanation: Crosslinks shorten trust paths.

questions.prompt: What is name resolution on an IP network?
choices: Converting a thread to a process.
Converting a file record to a cluster.
Converting a password to a salt.
Converting a computer name to an IP address.
questions.explanation: Name resolution maps names such as hostnames to IP addresses.

questions.prompt: Which Windows name-resolution methods are listed?
choices: WINS, broadcast name resolution, DNS, host file, and LMHOSTS file.
questions.explanation: Windows can use several name-resolution sources.

questions.prompt: What does WINS maintain?
choices: A dynamic database of name-to-IP address bindings.
A list of NTFS compression units.
questions.explanation: WINS servers and clients provide dynamic name resolution.

questions.prompt: How can Windows processes share kernel objects?
choices: Only by copying the whole object file.
Handle inheritance, opening a named object, or DuplicateHandle.
Only by rebooting into secure world.
questions.explanation: The Win32 API provides several handle-sharing mechanisms.

questions.prompt: What does CreateProcess do?
choices: Only opens a remote SMB file.
Loads dynamic link libraries used by the process and creates a primary thread.
questions.explanation: CreateProcess starts a process and its first thread.

questions.prompt: What does CreateThread do?
choices: Creates additional threads in a process.
questions.explanation: CreateThread adds execution units.

questions.prompt: Which Win32 priority class is typical for most processes?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists NORMAL as the typical class.

questions.prompt: What happens when a NORMAL_PRIORITY_CLASS process moves to the foreground?
choices: Its scheduling quantum is increased, often by a factor such as 3.
questions.explanation: Foreground boosting improves interactive response.

questions.prompt: How does Windows dynamically adjust thread priority?
choices: It never changes priority after creation.
It only uses file extension length.
It adjusts based on whether a thread is I/O-bound or CPU-bound.
questions.explanation: Dynamic boosts help balance responsiveness and CPU use.

questions.prompt: Which synchronization objects does the Windows kernel provide?
choices: Semaphores and mutexes, plus wait functions and critical sections in Win32.
questions.explanation: Windows provides several synchronization mechanisms for shared objects.

exams.title: Lecture 13 - OS for AI and Machine Learning
exams.description: Manual questions from lecture 13 slides.md, excluding the out-of-exam slide range.

questions.prompt: Why do AI and ML workloads need special OS support?
choices: They rely on massive parallelism, accelerators, extreme memory use, and high-throughput I/O.
They run only small sequential programs.
They never use GPUs or memory sharing.
They avoid all scheduling and drivers.
questions.explanation: AI workloads stress GPU scheduling, memory, and I/O differently from normal CPU-centric apps.

questions.prompt: Why are specialized drivers such as CUDA or ROCm important?
choices: They let AI software use GPU hardware efficiently for linear algebra operations.
They are only used for password hashing.
questions.explanation: Drivers bridge AI frameworks and accelerator hardware.

questions.prompt: Which workload must an AI-optimized OS coordinate across multi-GPU clusters?
choices: Thousands of concurrent threads and accelerator tasks while avoiding communication bottlenecks.
questions.explanation: Large GPU clusters need careful scheduling and communication placement.

questions.prompt: Why is unified memory useful for AI workloads?
choices: It prevents GPUs from accessing data.
It allows CPUs and GPUs to share memory/pooling and reduce copies or crashes.
questions.explanation: Unified memory helps high-throughput CPU-GPU data movement.

questions.prompt: What do agentic AI workflows require from OS-level infrastructure?
choices: Shared context, permissions, budgets, governance, auditing, and traceability.
A single isolated model with no state sharing.
questions.explanation: Multiple agents need safe shared state and governance.

questions.prompt: How does predictive resource allocation differ from traditional fair management?
choices: It only reacts after every process blocks.
It forecasts needs and proactively allocates cache, compute, storage, and memory.
It gives every process equal resources forever.
questions.explanation: AI workloads benefit from prediction instead of static heuristics.

questions.prompt: What is the architectural shift caused by LLMs and foundation models?
choices: From dynamic optimization to fixed single-task systems.
From CPU-centric static OS designs toward accelerator-centric self-optimizing platforms.
From resource management to no OS at all.
questions.explanation: Modern AI makes GPU/NPU the primary compute unit.

questions.prompt: How do traditional apps differ from AI workloads?
choices: Traditional apps always require more GPUs than AI.
AI workloads are always purely sequential.
AI workloads have predictable tiny memory access only.
Traditional apps are often control-flow or I/O bound; AI workloads are compute-bound and massively parallel.
questions.explanation: AI commonly uses GEMM and stochastic massive parallelism.

questions.prompt: What operation consumes much GPU time in ML workloads?
choices: Dense matrix multiplication such as GEMM.
questions.explanation: GEMM can consume 60 to 80 percent of GPU time.

questions.prompt: What is the memory wall in LLM systems?
choices: A firewall that blocks all network traffic.
A bottleneck where memory bandwidth and capacity limit latency and model serving.
A disk partition used for swap.
questions.explanation: LLMs can be limited by moving and storing KV/cache data more than raw compute.

questions.prompt: What does a KV cache store?
choices: Intermediate key and value vectors used to avoid recomputing attention over previous tokens.
questions.explanation: KV cache speeds autoregressive generation.

questions.prompt: How does KV cache memory usage scale?
choices: Always stays constant regardless of input.
Only with number of CPU cores.
Linearly with sequence length and batch size.
questions.explanation: Longer contexts and larger batches require more KV memory.

questions.prompt: What is accelerator-centric scheduling concerned with?
choices: Interconnect bandwidth, HBM saturation, and training vs inference priorities.
questions.explanation: Scheduling must match hardware limits such as NVLink and high-bandwidth memory.

questions.prompt: What is the main priority for AI training workloads?
choices: Lowest Time to First Token only.
High throughput such as tokens per second.
questions.explanation: Training aims to process as much work as possible.

questions.prompt: What is the main priority for inference serving?
choices: Low Time to First Token and responsiveness.
questions.explanation: Interactive inference cares about latency.

questions.prompt: Which frameworks are examples of large-scale AI training infrastructure?
questions.explanation: These frameworks manage huge models with parallelism.

questions.prompt: Which inference engines are named for optimizing responsiveness?
questions.explanation: They use advanced memory techniques for serving.

questions.prompt: What does resource management in AI systems optimize?
choices: Compute hardware, data, human capital, cost, performance, and energy.
questions.explanation: AI resource management balances complex infrastructure demands.

questions.prompt: Which hardware types are common in AI compute management?
questions.explanation: AI systems rely on heterogeneous accelerators.

questions.prompt: What is green scheduling?
choices: Always using the nearest deadline first.
Routing workloads to lower-carbon times or data centers and power-gating idle nodes.
questions.explanation: Energy-aware scheduling reduces power and carbon cost.

questions.prompt: Why is data pipelining important for AI?
choices: It prevents GPUs from being starved while streaming data and model updates.
questions.explanation: AI accelerators need steady data feeds.

questions.prompt: What does Kubernetes provide in AI orchestration?
choices: Container orchestration, job management, fault tolerance, and scaling support.
questions.explanation: Kubernetes manages distributed containerized workloads.

questions.prompt: How can reinforcement learning help resource allocation?
choices: An RL agent observes system state and learns sequential allocation decisions.
questions.explanation: RL can outperform fixed heuristics for changing workloads.

questions.prompt: What does predictive analytics do in data centers?
choices: Run every model on one CPU.
Forecast future resource demand from historical patterns to pre-provision hardware.
questions.explanation: Prediction helps avoid demand spikes.

questions.prompt: What is serverless computing in this context?
choices: Breaking AI functions into microservices that scale on demand and to zero when idle.
Installing one monolithic VM per user.
questions.explanation: Serverless reduces idle cost by allocating resources only when needed.

questions.prompt: What challenge does heterogeneity create?
choices: Mixed GPU/TPU/NPU generations have different performance and power profiles.
Every model fits on one device.
questions.explanation: Schedulers must account for diverse hardware.

questions.prompt: What is the scalability challenge in distributed AI training?
choices: Keeping all training on one CPU core.
Balancing large-scale training with communication overhead and network bandwidth limits.
questions.explanation: More GPUs can add communication cost.

questions.prompt: What is the cold start problem?
choices: Predicting resources for new models or sudden traffic with little or no history.
questions.explanation: Cold starts lack prior data for prediction.

questions.prompt: What are compute bubbles?
choices: Idle periods during inter-GPU communication or memory synchronization.
Disk blocks that cannot be read.
questions.explanation: Bubbles waste GPU time during distributed training.

questions.prompt: What does Hummingbird exploit?
choices: Small compute bubbles to run low-priority tasks without violating high-priority SLOs.
questions.explanation: Hummingbird uses microsecond-scale preemption opportunities.

questions.prompt: What does Bless do?
choices: Forces every GPU to run one tenant only.
Groups kernels from different applications to fill resource gaps and guarantee tenant quotas.
questions.explanation: Bless performs adaptive bubbleless sharing.

questions.prompt: Why must AI scheduling be NUMA-aware?
choices: To make all memory local by definition.
To reduce latency for cross-device transfers in complex hardware topologies.
questions.explanation: NUMA placement affects data-transfer cost.

questions.prompt: Why is naive KV-cache memory allocation wasteful?
choices: Internal fragmentation can waste 60 to 80 percent of memory.
It always reduces waste below 1 percent.
questions.explanation: Poor allocation leaves unusable gaps.

questions.prompt: What is PagedAttention?
choices: A virtual-memory-inspired method that partitions KV cache into fixed-size non-contiguous blocks.
questions.explanation: PagedAttention reduces fragmentation and supports prefix sharing.

questions.prompt: What benefit does PagedAttention provide?
choices: It removes the KV cache completely.
It can reduce KV-cache fragmentation to under 4 percent and share common prefixes.
questions.explanation: Paged KV blocks improve memory efficiency.

questions.prompt: What does IceCache do?
choices: Groups semantically related tokens into the same memory pages to improve retrieval and bandwidth use.
questions.explanation: Semantic clustering can increase cache hit rates.

questions.prompt: What does KV-cache offloading with NVIDIA Dynamo or LMCache enable?
choices: Moving KV cache to CPU RAM or SSD so very long contexts can be served with fewer GPUs.
Making every token recompute from scratch.
questions.explanation: Offloading extends context capacity beyond GPU memory.

questions.prompt: Which tier is primary for active AI computation in tiered memory?
questions.explanation: HBM is closest to GPU compute and highest bandwidth.

questions.prompt: What is Stream2LLM designed to reduce?
choices: Time-to-First-Token by overlapping context streaming and prefill.
questions.explanation: Stream2LLM targets long-context I/O latency.

questions.prompt: What role do GPU drivers play?
choices: Translate high-level APIs into hardware commands and manage queues, memory, scheduling, and interrupts.
questions.explanation: Drivers bridge CUDA/Vulkan/DirectX APIs and GPU hardware.

questions.prompt: Why are GPU device queues often ring buffers?
choices: CPU and GPU can share command queues through DMA-accessible memory.
They store passwords in circular form.
questions.explanation: Ring buffers support asynchronous producer-consumer command flow.

questions.prompt: What is the split between user-space and kernel-space GPU drivers?
choices: User-space handles interrupts; kernel-space draws only windows.
User-space translates API calls; kernel-space manages memory, scheduling, interrupts, and hardware communication.
Both parts do exactly the same work.
questions.explanation: Modern drivers split high-level translation from privileged hardware control.

questions.prompt: What does DCGM provide in GPU clusters?
choices: Health tracking, telemetry, and thermal reporting.
questions.explanation: NVIDIA DCGM is a management suite over the driver stack.

questions.prompt: What is GPU direct pass-through?
choices: Splitting GPU silicon into many isolated MIG instances.
Sharing one GPU through time slices only.
Assigning a full physical GPU exclusively to one VM for near-native performance.
questions.explanation: Pass-through is fast but not flexible because the GPU is not shared.

questions.prompt: What is API remoting?
choices: Capturing API calls such as CUDA in a container and forwarding them to a host GPU.
Giving the VM an entire physical GPU.
Slicing GPU hardware at silicon level.
questions.explanation: API remoting is lightweight but may lack native feature coverage.

questions.prompt: What is mediated pass-through or vGPU?
choices: A full GPU is dedicated to one VM only.
A hypervisor abstracts a physical GPU into virtual instances using a split driver model.
Every CUDA call is sent by email.
The CPU performs all graphics work.
questions.explanation: vGPU shares a physical GPU among VMs.

questions.prompt: How does MIG differ from time-slicing?
choices: MIG slices GPU hardware into isolated compute and memory partitions, while time-slicing rapidly switches contexts.
questions.explanation: MIG provides stronger hardware-level partitioning.

questions.prompt: What are MMIO and BARs used for?
choices: They store KV-cache tokens by semantic class only.
They let the CPU access GPU control registers through mapped memory windows.
questions.explanation: BARs expose device memory/control regions to the CPU.

questions.prompt: What does DMA allow the GPU to do?
choices: Read data directly from system RAM without the CPU manually copying every byte.
questions.explanation: DMA improves high-speed data movement.

questions.prompt: What are command buffers?
choices: Packaged instructions that the GPU reads asynchronously through DMA.
questions.explanation: CPU drivers submit GPU work through command buffers.

questions.prompt: Why are semaphores, fences, and barriers needed in GPU systems?
choices: They coordinate asynchronous CPU-GPU actions and prevent data races.
questions.explanation: Synchronization primitives order work across devices.

questions.prompt: What is distributed AI?
choices: Running every model on one isolated laptop only.
Using only a single GPU with no network.
Spreading AI training or inference across multiple interconnected nodes or devices.
questions.explanation: Distributed AI lets devices collaborate and process data locally or jointly.

questions.prompt: What metrics measure distributed training success?
choices: Model FLOPs Utilization and training throughput.
questions.explanation: MFU and throughput reflect training efficiency.

questions.prompt: What is data parallelism?
choices: Splitting a single matrix multiplication across devices.
Replicating the model while each device processes a different data batch.
Placing different layers on different devices.
questions.explanation: DP distributes batches across model replicas.

questions.prompt: What does ZeRO shard?
choices: Optimizer states, gradients, and parameters depending on stage.
questions.explanation: ZeRO reduces redundant memory across devices.

questions.prompt: What is tensor parallelism?
choices: Replicating the full model per data batch.
Sharding individual layers or operators such as matrix multiplications across GPUs.
questions.explanation: TP splits compute inside layers.

questions.prompt: Why does tensor parallelism need high-bandwidth links?
choices: It is communication-intensive between GPUs working on the same operator.
questions.explanation: NVLink-like intranode bandwidth is important for TP.

questions.prompt: What is pipeline parallelism?
choices: Replicating all layers on every device.
Splitting model layers vertically across devices.
questions.explanation: PP assigns different stages of the model to different devices.

questions.prompt: What problem does pipeline parallelism introduce?
choices: Pipeline bubbles where devices wait for micro-batches.
questions.explanation: Bubbles occur when stages are idle.

questions.prompt: What is context parallelism?
choices: Splitting GPU silicon into MIG instances.
Replicating the whole model for each batch.
Forwarding API calls to a host GPU.
Partitioning the sequence dimension to support ultra-long contexts.
questions.explanation: CP distributes activation memory for long contexts.

questions.prompt: What does 4D hybrid parallelism combine?
choices: Data, pipeline, tensor, and context parallelism.
questions.explanation: Modern training uses multiple parallelism dimensions together.

questions.prompt: Which collective aggregates slices from all ranks?
questions.explanation: AllGather collects pieces so all ranks receive the full result.

questions.prompt: Which collective reduces data and scatters results?
questions.explanation: ReduceScatter combines reduction with distribution.

questions.prompt: Which collective is essential for Mixture-of-Experts routing?
questions.explanation: MoE often routes tokens between many ranks.

questions.prompt: What is the role of Ray in AI orchestration?
choices: Scaling workloads and coordinating distributed processes.
questions.explanation: Ray is a framework for distributed AI workload coordination.

questions.prompt: What is a sparing strategy in fault tolerance?
choices: Pre-allocating spare compute blocks to replace failed nodes quickly.
Waiting for manual hardware repair before continuing.
questions.explanation: Spares reduce downtime in large training runs.

questions.prompt: What does NodeSweep help with?
choices: Straggler detection and node health management.
questions.explanation: NodeSweep monitors bad or slow nodes.

questions.prompt: Why do containers matter for AI?
choices: They always provide stronger hardware isolation than VMs.
They cannot run on cloud infrastructure.
They provide reproducible, portable, lightweight environments with packaged frameworks and drivers.
questions.explanation: Containers solve environment consistency and deployment portability.

questions.prompt: What does the NVIDIA Container Toolkit enable?
choices: Docker containers can access host GPU resources through the driver.
KV cache is stored only on SSD.
questions.explanation: The toolkit connects containers to NVIDIA GPUs.

questions.prompt: What is Edge AI?
choices: Running all AI only in far cloud datacenters.
Running AI models near the data source instead of only in centralized cloud servers.
questions.explanation: Edge AI reduces latency and can improve privacy.

questions.prompt: Why are TinyML and Small Language Models useful at the edge?
choices: They require the largest possible cloud GPUs.
They reduce latency and improve privacy on constrained devices.
questions.explanation: Small models fit local hardware better.

questions.prompt: What does TinyML require according to the lecture?
choices: Aggressive model compression and integer-only attention pipelines.
questions.explanation: TinyML targets very constrained devices.

questions.prompt: What is OrinFlight designed for?
choices: Real-time embedded AI on NVIDIA Jetson Orin for autonomous aviation.
questions.explanation: OrinFlight targets strict real-time embedded AI.

questions.prompt: Which scheduling/security features does OrinFlight include?
choices: Synchronized distributed processing, priority-based CPU/GPU scheduling, and SELinux.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists these as OrinFlight features.

questions.prompt: What is CognitiveOS?
choices: A disk file system for Linux.
An LMM-based robotic system that integrates multimodal generative AI into the control loop.
questions.explanation: CognitiveOS connects generative AI with robotic control.

questions.prompt: Which resource type can be taken away without causing failure?
questions.explanation: Preemptable resources, such as CPU or memory frames, can be reclaimed safely.

questions.prompt: Which deadlock condition means a process keeps allocated resources while requesting more?
questions.explanation: Hold and wait is when a process already holds resources and waits for additional ones.

questions.prompt: In a resource-allocation graph, what does a cycle indicate when each resource type has one instance?
questions.explanation: With one instance per resource type, a cycle is sufficient to show deadlock.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the Banker's algorithm?
choices: Grant a request only if the resulting state remains safe
questions.explanation: Banker's algorithm avoids unsafe allocations by checking for a safe sequence.

questions.prompt: What does an unsafe state mean?
choices: There is no guarantee that all processes can finish without deadlock
Deadlock has already happened in every case
questions.explanation: Unsafe does not always mean deadlocked now, but it means deadlock cannot be ruled out.

questions.prompt: ما المقصود بالاستبعاد المتبادل في شروط الجمود؟
choices: مورد لا يمكن أن يستخدمه أكثر من process في نفس الوقت
كل process يمتلك كل الموارد دائما
questions.explanation: الاستبعاد المتبادل يعني أن المورد غير قابل للمشاركة في اللحظة نفسها.

questions.prompt: ما معنى الانتظار الدائري؟
choices: كل process في سلسلة ينتظر موردا تمسكه العملية التالية
كل process يعمل وحده ولا ينتظر
questions.explanation: الانتظار الدائري هو أحد الشروط الأربعة اللازمة للجمود.

questions.prompt: في خوارزمية المصرفي، متى يتم قبول الطلب؟
choices: إذا لم يعلن process احتياجه الأقصى
إذا بقيت الحالة آمنة بعد منحه
questions.explanation: المصرفي يمنح الطلب فقط إذا بقي ترتيب آمن لإكمال العمليات.

exams.title: Algorithm - Page Replacement
exams.description: Manual questions from page-replacement.txt and page-replacement Markdown explanations.

questions.prompt: Which page replacement algorithm removes the page that will not be used for the longest future time?
questions.explanation: Optimal replacement chooses the page whose next use is farthest in the future.

questions.prompt: Which algorithm may suffer from Belady's anomaly?
questions.explanation: FIFO can produce more page faults when more frames are added in some reference strings.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of LRU?
choices: Replace the page that has not been used for the longest time
questions.explanation: LRU uses past recent use as a prediction of future use.

questions.prompt: What does Second Chance add to FIFO?
choices: A reference bit check before replacing the oldest page
questions.explanation: Second Chance skips recently referenced pages by clearing their reference bit.

questions.prompt: How does FCFS choose the next process?
choices: A random lottery ticket is drawn
The process with the largest memory use always runs first
The process that arrived first runs first
The process with the shortest remaining time always runs first
questions.explanation: First-Come, First-Served follows arrival order.

questions.prompt: What is a common disadvantage of FCFS?
choices: It always gives the best response time
A long job can make shorter jobs wait behind it
It requires future knowledge of all jobs
It cannot be implemented with a queue
questions.explanation: This is the convoy effect.

questions.prompt: For FCFS, waiting time for a process depends mainly on what?
choices: The burst times of processes scheduled before it
The number of page faults only
The size of the file name
questions.explanation: A process waits until all earlier arrivals finish.

questions.prompt: What is the time quantum in Round-Robin scheduling?
choices: The number of files in a directory
The maximum CPU time a process gets before possible preemption
questions.explanation: Round-Robin uses a fixed time slice called a quantum.

questions.prompt: What happens if a process does not finish within its quantum?
choices: It gets all remaining CPU time
It is preempted and placed back in the ready queue
questions.explanation: The scheduler cycles to the next ready process.

questions.prompt: What is the tradeoff in choosing a Round-Robin quantum?
choices: Small quantum improves response but increases context-switch overhead
Small quantum removes all context switches
Large quantum guarantees fairness for interactive tasks
questions.explanation: Quantum size balances responsiveness and overhead.

questions.prompt: What is the role of the master in a master-worker MPI design?
choices: Distribute tasks to workers and collect results
Replace every worker with a page frame
questions.explanation: The master coordinates work distribution and result collection.

questions.prompt: What is the role of a worker in a master-worker design?
choices: Receive assigned work, process it, and return the result
questions.explanation: Workers perform the assigned computation.

questions.prompt: What do sockets provide in a distributed master-worker program?
choices: A local-only mutex with no messages
A communication endpoint for sending and receiving data over a network
A replacement for all CPU scheduling
questions.explanation: Sockets are used for network communication between processes.

questions.prompt: Why is message format important in socket-based coordination?
choices: Both sides must interpret tasks and results consistently
questions.explanation: A clear protocol prevents misunderstanding between master and worker.

exams.title: Algorithm - شرح خوارزميات الاستبدال
exams.description: Manual questions from خوازميات المحاضرة الرابعة.md and related Arabic algorithm explanations.

questions.prompt: في خوارزمية الاستبدال المثالية، أي صفحة يتم اختيارها للإزالة؟
choices: الصفحة التي لن تستخدم لأطول فترة في المستقبل
questions.explanation: الخوارزمية المثالية تعتمد على معرفة الاستخدام المستقبلي.

questions.prompt: في FIFO، ما الصفحة التي تزال عند حدوث page fault؟
choices: الصفحة التي لم تدخل الذاكرة بعد
questions.explanation: FIFO يتبع ترتيب دخول الصفحات إلى الذاكرة.

questions.prompt: ما الفكرة الأساسية في NRU؟
choices: إرسال المهام من master إلى worker
تصنيف الصفحات حسب بتات الاستخدام والتعديل واختيار فئة منخفضة
منح الموارد فقط في الحالة الآمنة
questions.explanation: NRU يستخدم معلومات R وM لاختيار صفحة مناسبة للاستبدال.
